proposals.title: The Ambient Store: A Longitudinal Field Study on the Dynamic Interplay of Sensory Cues, Social Density, and Consumer Mindset Using Ecological Momentary Assessment
proposals.motivation: Consumer research has long recognized the impact of store environment on behavior, yet validation often relies on lab experiments or retrospective surveys, which lack ecological validity and temporal granularity. Pre-2026, the rise of mobile technology and sensor data creates an opportunity to study real-time, in-situ consumer experiences, addressing a critical gap in understanding how dynamic environmental factors shape decisions. This aligns with the growing need for rigorous, multi-method validation in marketing science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effect of specific sensory cues (e.g., scent, music tempo) on consumer engagement and purchase intention is moderated by real-time social density and dynamically mediated by the consumer's momentary affective and cognitive mindset.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal field study in a partner retail store, employing Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA). Participants will be recruited via a store app and prompted at randomized intervals during their visit to report affective state and perceptions via their smartphones, linked to time-stamped behavioral data (e.g., dwell time, path). We will instrument the store with environmental sensors to log objective measures of social density (via anonymized WiFi/BLE pings) and controlled sensory manipulations. Data analysis will use multi-level structural equation modeling (SEM) and latent growth curve models to test dynamic mediation and moderation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, ecologically valid framework for modeling the real-time person-environment interaction in retail, moving beyond static models. It will contribute methodologically by demonstrating how EMA and sensor data can be integrated for calibration and validation of consumer mindset metrics, extending Sarstedt's prior work on measurement validation and mining user-generated content to the physical realm.
proposals.novelty: While environmental psychology methods are established, their application for *dynamic*, *within-individual* analysis of commercial settings using integrated sensor-EMA data is novel in consumer research. This moves beyond correlating store atmospherics with aggregate outcomes to modeling the momentary psychological process.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Model of Multisensory Integration for Predicting Consumer Perception of Brand Experiences
proposals.motivation: Consumer perception is inherently multisensory, yet marketing science often relies on single-modality measures or simple additive models. Pre-2026 research in perceptual psychology has established Bayesian causal inference as a powerful framework for modeling how the brain integrates ambiguous signals from different senses, but its application to consumer contexts like product evaluation or brand experience remains nascent. This gap limits our ability to predict how consumers form holistic perceptions from complex, multi-channel marketing stimuli.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian causal inference model, which accounts for the perceived unity of sensory sources, will outperform traditional linear models in predicting consumer perception and preference for multisensory brand stimuli (e.g., packaging, retail environments, digital ads).
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled lab experiments where participants evaluate stimuli varying in visual (e.g., color, shape), auditory (e.g., sound), and tactile properties. Using hierarchical Bayesian modeling, we will fit a multisensory integration model where the brain infers whether sensory cues originate from a common source (the brand) before integrating them. The model's parameters (e.g., sensory reliabilities, prior for common cause) will be estimated using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling via Stan. Model predictions for perceived quality and liking will be compared against linear regression and ANOVA baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, psychologically-grounded computational model for multisensory consumer perception, moving beyond descriptive accounts. It will offer marketers a principled method to design coherent multisensory experiences by quantifying cue reliability and unity. The project will also demonstrate the value of importing rigorous perceptual models into consumer research for enhanced measurement and prediction.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian models exist in perceptual science, their adaptation to model *brand* as a inferred common cause of multiple sensory cues is novel. This bridges a core marketing construct with a formal computational mechanism from psychology, offering a non-obvious, theory-driven alternative to data-mining approaches for sensory marketing.

proposals.title: Optimizing the Sensory Retailscape: A Conjoint-Based Framework for Decomposing and Validating Atmospherics Configurations
proposals.motivation: Retail atmospherics are a critical driver of consumer behavior, yet their design often relies on intuition or piecemeal testing. Pre-2026, the field lacks a systematic, consumer-centric methodology to decompose and optimize the complex, multi-sensory attribute combinations that define the in-store experience. This gap limits retailers' ability to create evidence-based, optimally engaging environments in an increasingly competitive omnichannel landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a choice-based conjoint analysis, adapted for sensory retail attributes, can reliably identify the part-worth utilities of specific atmospherics elements (e.g., lighting hue, scent intensity, music genre, texture of fixtures) and reveal synergistic or antagonistic interactions between them, leading to a quantifiably optimal configuration.
proposals.method: We will employ a hierarchical Bayesian choice-based conjoint design. Participants will evaluate realistic, digitally simulated retail scenarios (using VR or high-fidelity renders) where sensory attributes are systematically varied according to a D-efficient experimental design. Choice data will be analyzed using a Multinomial Logit model, with estimation performed via Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) simulation in Stan to derive individual-level part-worth utilities. The optimal configuration will be validated through a calibration experiment, comparing choice predictions against stated preferences in a controlled lab setting, as per Sarstedt's validation framework.
proposals.contribution: This research provides a novel, generalizable framework for the evidence-based design of retail atmospherics, moving beyond single-attribute studies. It contributes methodologically by integrating conjoint analysis with sensory marketing and validation techniques, and managerially by offering a tool to maximize the return on investment in store environment design.
proposals.novelty: While conjoint analysis is established in product design, its rigorous application to decompose the 'product' of the retail atmosphere itself—a holistic, immersive sensory experience—is novel. The integration with calibration experiments for validation addresses a key methodological gap in atmospherics research identified in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Head-to-Head Comparison: Calibration Experiments Versus the Multitrait-Multimethod Matrix for Measurement Validation in Consumer Research
proposals.motivation: Valid measurement is foundational for consumer research, yet the dominant validation paradigm, the Multitrait-Multimethod (MTMM) matrix, faces persistent critiques regarding its practical implementation and interpretation. The recent introduction of calibration experiments offers a promising alternative, but its relative empirical performance against MTMM remains untested. A direct comparison is crucial to guide methodological choices and advance measurement science in marketing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that calibration experiments will demonstrate superior diagnostic specificity in identifying measurement flaws (e.g., method bias, item non-equivalence) compared to MTMM analysis, while MTMM will provide more comprehensive convergent and discriminant validity estimates for well-behaved constructs.
proposals.method: We will conduct two online experimental studies measuring established consumer constructs (e.g., brand engagement, perceived risk) using multiple methods (e.g., reflective scales, formative indices, sensory descriptions). For each dataset, we will perform both a full MTMM analysis using covariance-based structural equation modeling (CB-SEM) with correlated uniqueness models and a series of calibration experiments employing ANOVA and regression-based moderation tests. Performance will be compared on criteria including power to detect known method biases (introduced via experimental manipulation), Type I error rates, and practical interpretability of results.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first empirical benchmark comparing these two validation frameworks, offering concrete, evidence-based guidelines for researchers. It will clarify the specific strengths and weaknesses of each approach, moving the field beyond theoretical debate toward informed methodological application.
proposals.novelty: While both methods are established, no prior work has subjected them to a controlled, head-to-head empirical test on the same measurement problems, which is necessary to move from philosophical preference to scientific recommendation.

proposals.title: Calibrating the Wild: Extending the Calibration Framework for Measurement Validation in Field and Longitudinal Consumer Research
proposals.motivation: Sarstedt's (2024) calibration framework offers a powerful alternative to multi-method validation but is confined to controlled, cross-sectional experiments. Consumer behavior, however, is dynamic and context-dependent, measured increasingly through field data (e.g., social media, sensors) and longitudinal panels. Pre-2026, a critical gap exists in validating such 'in-the-wild' measurements, which are prone to context-specific biases and temporal instability, threatening the validity of substantive conclusions drawn from them.
proposals.hypothesis: The core calibration framework can be successfully adapted to field and longitudinal settings, providing a robust, context-sensitive method for validating consumer mindset and behavioral metrics outside the lab. Specifically, we hypothesize that incorporating temporal and contextual moderators into the calibration model will significantly improve its diagnostic accuracy for real-world data.
proposals.method: We will extend the original latent variable calibration model to a multi-group, multi-occasion structural equation modeling (SEM) framework. For field settings, we implement a multi-group SEM where 'groups' represent different natural contexts (e.g., brand pages, review platforms), testing for measurement invariance. For longitudinal data, we use latent growth curve models and latent state-trait models within the calibration framework to separate trait variance from occasion-specific measurement error. Validation will use pre-2026 datasets: longitudinal panel data on brand engagement and a multi-platform corpus of user-generated content, applying algorithms for natural language processing (e.g., LIWC, BERT embeddings) to generate the target metrics.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a methodological bridge, enabling researchers to rigorously validate measurements obtained in ecologically valid but noisy environments. It contributes directly to the methodological toolkit for consumer neuroscience, social media analytics, and longitudinal studies, enhancing confidence in findings from these growing research streams. The output will be a formalized protocol for 'field calibration'.
proposals.novelty: While calibration and longitudinal SEM exist, their integration for the specific purpose of measurement validation in uncontrolled settings is novel. This moves calibration from a static, lab-based diagnostic to a dynamic tool that accounts for the situational and temporal heterogeneity inherent in real consumer data.

proposals.title: Calibrating Consumer Sensitivity: A Signal Detection Theory Framework for Validating Marketing Constructs
proposals.motivation: Consumer research faces a persistent measurement validation challenge, as highlighted by Sarstedt's recent work on calibration experiments. Traditional multi-method approaches often fail to quantify a measure's fundamental sensitivity to the specific experimental manipulations intended to change it. This limits the field's ability to establish robust, causally interpretable constructs, hindering theory development and practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying psychophysical signal detection theory (SDT) to consumer calibration experiments will yield a quantifiable sensitivity index (d') that is superior to traditional difference-in-means tests for validating whether a measurement instrument is genuinely sensitive to its target theoretical construct.
proposals.method: We will design calibration experiments where the intensity of a theoretical construct (e.g., brand engagement) is systematically manipulated across multiple levels (e.g., low, medium, high) via validated scenarios. Participant responses on target scales (e.g., brand attitude) will be analyzed using SDT. Specifically, we will compute d' (sensitivity) and criterion (bias) parameters for each adjacent manipulation level pair, treating one level as 'noise' and the next as 'signal'. This creates a sensitivity profile for the scale across the construct's intensity range. We will compare the diagnostic power of d' against ANOVA-based effect sizes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a formal, theory-grounded method for quantifying measurement sensitivity, moving beyond simple detection to gauging the strength of a measure's response. It will offer a new standard for calibration experiments in consumer research, enhancing the rigor of construct validation. The resulting sensitivity profiles can inform scale refinement and selection for experimental studies.
proposals.novelty: While SDT is established in sensory science, its application to calibrate abstract marketing and consumer constructs is novel. This bridges a core method from psychophysics with contemporary validation debates, offering a non-obvious, quantitative lens for a problem typically addressed with qualitative or correlational techniques.

proposals.title: Designing for Causality: A Potential Outcomes Framework for Calibration Experiments in Consumer Research
proposals.motivation: Calibration experiments, as proposed by Sarstedt et al., are a promising alternative for measurement validation but rely on strong manipulation validity claims. In pre-2026 consumer research, establishing robust causal links between manipulations and latent constructs remains a key challenge, often addressed with multi-method approaches that are resource-intensive. A formal causal framework is needed to strengthen the internal validity of calibration designs, moving beyond correlational evidence to more defensible causal claims about measurement instruments.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a design-based causal inference framework (specifically, the potential outcomes model) to the planning and analysis of calibration experiments will yield stronger, more statistically rigorous evidence for manipulation validity than traditional analytical approaches. This will be evidenced by clearer identification of causal assumptions and more precise estimation of the causal effect of the manipulation on the target latent construct.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Rubin Causal Model (RCM) to the structure of calibration experiments. The experimental manipulation (e.g., a sensory prime) is treated as the 'treatment,' and the target latent construct score is the primary outcome. We will employ design-based principles, emphasizing randomization of stimuli or task order to satisfy the Ignorability assumption. Analysis will utilize randomization-based inference or Bayesian structural models to estimate the Average Causal Effect (ACE) of the manipulation. Sensitivity analyses (e.g., using the Rosenbaum bounds framework) will be conducted to assess the robustness of the causal claim to potential unobserved confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a formal methodological bridge between causal inference and psychometric validation, offering consumer researchers a principled toolkit for designing and analyzing calibration experiments. It will enhance the credibility of manipulation validity claims, potentially reducing the need for exhaustive multi-method triangulation. The proposal contributes directly to the methodological literature on measurement validation and causal identification in experimental consumer science.
proposals.novelty: While causal inference is established in econometrics and social sciences, its explicit integration into the design and validation logic of calibration experiments is novel. This moves calibration from a primarily correlational/descriptive framework to a causally-grounded one, addressing a core validity threat that existing multi-method approaches aim to solve but through a different, formalized lens.

proposals.title: Calibrating Mindsets: Integrating Diagnostic Classification Models into Consumer Measurement Validation
proposals.motivation: Consumer research increasingly relies on latent constructs measured with multi-item scales, yet validation methods like calibration experiments (Sarstedt et al., 2024) primarily assess nomological validity at the scale level. This overlooks the diagnostic quality of individual items and the potential for heterogeneous consumer profiles. Pre-2026 psychometrics offers advanced models like Diagnostic Classification Models (DCMs) that can profile respondents based on mastery of specific, fine-grained attributes, providing a more nuanced validation framework.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating Diagnostic Classification Models (DCMs) into the calibration experiment framework will yield superior diagnostic information about measurement quality, identifying not only whether a scale is valid but also which specific latent attributes are measured by which items and for which consumer segments.
proposals.method: We will extend the calibration experiment design by administering multiple established scales for a target construct (e.g., brand engagement) alongside behavioral outcome measures. Instead of only using factor analysis, we will apply a two-step modeling approach: First, we estimate a DCM (e.g., using the GDINA package in R) to classify respondents into profiles based on their pattern of responses to all scale items. Second, we test the differential predictive validity of these latent profiles on the behavioral outcomes within the calibration framework, using ANOVA and post-hoc tests. This quantifies how well specific attribute mastery profiles predict criterion variables.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, item-level diagnostic tool for measurement validation in consumer research, moving beyond global scale assessment. It will offer a methodological bridge between psychometrics and consumer science, enabling researchers to refine scales by identifying non-informative items and to segment consumers based on their underlying psychological attribute mastery.
proposals.novelty: While DCMs are established in educational testing, their application to validate and refine marketing and consumer psychology scales is novel. This integration moves calibration from assessing 'does the scale work?' to diagnosing 'exactly what does each item work for, and for whom?'

proposals.title: From Expression to Connection: Testing the Causal Effect of eWOM Generation on Self-Brand Connection Strength
proposals.motivation: Consumer research often posits self-brand connection (SBC) as an antecedent to positive behaviors like electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM). This unidirectional view overlooks the potential for the act of eWOM generation itself to reinforce or even create the very connection it is assumed to express. Understanding this reversed causality is crucial for marketing theory and practice, as it suggests that encouraging user-generated content could be a powerful tool for building brand relationships, not just a consequence of them.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the act of generating eWOM about a brand causally strengthens the individual's self-brand connection, controlling for prior attitudes. This effect will be moderated by the valence (positive/negative) and public visibility of the eWOM act.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method approach, integrating controlled experiments and field data. First, a longitudinal panel experiment will manipulate eWOM creation tasks (e.g., writing a review vs. passive reading) and measure SBC changes over time. Second, we will analyze naturalistic user-generated content (UGC) from social media brand pages using established text-mining techniques (e.g., LIWC, topic modeling) and computational sentiment analysis to infer SBC markers from language, then apply cross-lagged panel models (CLPM) to the time-series data to test for temporal precedence and Granger causality.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge and refine the dominant causal model in the SBC and engagement literature by empirically testing a reversed mediation pathway. It will provide managers with evidence that facilitating eWOM creation is not merely an outcome metric but an active relationship-building lever. Methodologically, it extends Sarstedt's recent work on calibration and UGC mining by applying these techniques to a novel causal question.
proposals.novelty: The proposal directly inverts the established SBC → eWOM causal direction, a non-obvious proposition given the trait-like conceptualization of SBC in much prior work. It innovatively combines experimental causal evidence with longitudinal observational data from real UGC to robustly test this reversed pathway.

proposals.title: The Silent Bond: Measuring the Impact of Passive Social Media Brand Page Engagement on Brand Attachment and Loyalty
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 marketing science and social media research overwhelmingly focuses on active engagement metrics (likes, shares, comments) as indicators of brand strength. However, the vast majority of consumer interactions with brand content are passive (e.g., scrolling, viewing). This creates a critical gap in understanding how these prevalent but unmeasured passive behaviors contribute to brand relationship formation, potentially misguiding resource allocation and strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: Passive consumption of brand-related content on social media platforms (e.g., viewing posts, stories, videos) has a stronger direct effect on brand attachment and loyalty than active engagement behaviors, mediated by the effortless accumulation of sensory and cognitive brand capital.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-method calibration study. First, we will mine user-generated content and behavioral trace data (via platform APIs, where permissible) to develop latent metrics of passive engagement (e.g., dwell time, scroll speed, content exposure frequency) using natural language processing and computer vision algorithms (e.g., BERT for text, OpenCV for video analysis). Second, we will conduct a controlled experiment manipulating exposure to brand content, measuring neural and physiological responses (EEG, eye-tracking) as indicators of passive processing. These measures will be calibrated against self-reported brand attachment and loyalty in a structural equation model.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated framework for measuring passive engagement, shifting the paradigm from an activity-centric to an exposure-centric view of social media marketing. It will offer managers evidence-based strategies to design content for passive consumption, optimizing for brand capital accumulation rather than mere interaction rates.
proposals.novelty: While prior work acknowledges lurkers, this study is the first to theorize passive engagement as a primary, measurable driver of brand ties and to empirically calibrate its impact against active participation using neuroscientific and trace data methods.

proposals.title: Beyond the Mainstream: Personality-Driven Engagement on Niche Social Platforms
proposals.motivation: Consumer research on social media marketing and electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM) is overwhelmingly focused on major platforms like Facebook, Instagram, and Twitter. This creates a significant gap in understanding consumer behavior in specialized online communities (e.g., Goodreads, Strava, DeviantArt, niche forums). The psychological drivers of engagement and content generation on these platforms, where shared interests supersede general social connectivity, are likely distinct, limiting the generalizability of existing models.
proposals.hypothesis: The established positive relationship between extraversion and brand page engagement/eWOM generation found on mainstream social networks is attenuated or reversed on niche social platforms, where conscientiousness or openness to experience become more salient predictors of user participation.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method validation approach, combining survey data with user-generated content (UGC) mining. First, we will recruit users from 2-3 niche platforms via targeted sampling to measure personality (Big Five) and platform engagement. Second, we will computationally analyze the users' historical UGC (posts, reviews, logs) using established NLP techniques (e.g., LIWC for linguistic style, topic modeling via LDA for content themes) to create behavioral engagement metrics. Structural equation modeling (SEM) with latent variables will test the differential effects of personality traits, using the UGC-derived metrics for calibration and validation of self-reported outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a more nuanced, context-dependent theory of personality's role in social media engagement, moving beyond a 'one-size-fits-all' model. It will offer marketers and brand managers actionable insights for segmenting and targeting audiences on specialized platforms. Methodologically, it demonstrates the value of calibration experiments using UGC to validate psychological constructs in digital environments.
proposals.novelty: While the extraversion-engagement link is well-established for broad networks, this study explicitly challenges its universality by testing it in niche, interest-based digital spaces, a context largely ignored in pre-2026 literature. It uniquely combines survey-based personality assessment with the computational analysis of authentic behavioral traces (UGC) from these platforms.

proposals.title: Platform Personality: Testing the Generalizability of Extraversion's Role in Social Media eWOM Generation Across Instagram and TikTok
proposals.motivation: Sarstedt et al.'s (2024) finding that extraversion drives electronic word-of-mouth (eWONG) on Facebook brand pages is pivotal for targeted marketing. However, the rapid rise of visually and algorithmically distinct platforms like Instagram and TikTok necessitates testing this relationship's boundary conditions. Understanding whether platform affordances and user norms moderate the personality-eWONG link is crucial for developing robust, platform-agnostic consumer theory.
proposals.hypothesis: The positive effect of extraversion on eWONG generation through social media brand page engagement is moderated by platform type, being weaker on visually-centric platforms (e.g., Instagram) and short-form video platforms (e.g., TikTok) compared to text-and-network-centric platforms (e.g., Facebook).
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-platform calibration experiment and observational study. First, we will recruit a panel of consumers, measure their extraversion using the Ten-Item Personality Inventory (TIPI), and track their engagement with standardized brand content across Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok. Second, we will mine user-generated content and engagement metrics (likes, comments, shares) from public brand pages on these platforms, using established text and image analysis algorithms (e.g., LIWC, OpenCV for basic visual feature extraction) to infer behavioral proxies for eWONG. The data will be analyzed using multi-group structural equation modeling (SEM) to test for measurement invariance and moderation effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute to personality and social media literature by establishing boundary conditions for the extraversion-eWONG relationship. For practitioners, it will provide evidence-based guidance for tailoring content and influencer strategies to platform-specific user psychographics. It also extends Sarstedt's calibration approach to a novel, cross-platform validation context.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes the extraversion-eWONG link on single platforms, this is the first pre-planned, theory-driven test of its generalizability across fundamentally different platform ecosystems, integrating experimental calibration with mined behavioral data.

proposals.title: The Extraversion Divide: A Multi-Method Study on Personality as a Moderator of Positive and Negative Electronic Word-of-Mouth Generation
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Sarstedt's own work, establishes extraversion as a key driver of overall electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM) volume. However, in the pre-2026 landscape, a critical gap exists: it remains unclear whether extraversion uniformly increases both positive and negative eWOM, or if it differentially influences valence. This distinction is vital for brand management and social media strategy, as the consequences of amplified negative versus positive eWOM are profoundly different.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while extraversion generally increases eWOM generation, it has a stronger positive effect on the generation of positive eWOM compared to negative eWOM, due to extraverts' greater social approval motivation and tendency to maintain positive self-presentation.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-method validation approach. First, a controlled calibration experiment will manipulate brand engagement (high/low) and measure subsequent eWOM valence, with extraversion as a measured moderator. Second, to test ecological validity, we will mine user-generated content from social media brand pages using a pre-trained BERT model fine-tuned for sentiment and extraversion detection (based on linguistic cues validated in pre-2026 psycholinguistics). The causal path from engagement to valence, moderated by extraversion, will be tested using multi-group structural equation modeling (SEM) in both datasets.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a more nuanced understanding of personality's role in eWOM, moving beyond volume to predict valence. It will offer managers actionable insights for segmenting communication strategies on social media based on consumer personality signals. Methodologically, it demonstrates the value of combining calibration experiments with mined user-generated content for robust measurement and validation.
proposals.novelty: This study is the first to theorize and test a differential moderating effect of extraversion on eWOM valence, challenging the implicit assumption that drivers of eWOM volume affect all valence types equally. It integrates experimental causality with large-scale behavioral data from social media.

proposals.title: Beyond Clicks and Likes: A Psychophysiological Calibration of Implicit Engagement on Social Media Brand Pages
proposals.motivation: Consumer engagement on social media brand pages is predominantly measured through explicit, self-reported metrics or behavioral proxies (e.g., likes, shares), which are susceptible to social desirability and do not capture the automatic, affective drivers of behavior. This gap is critical, as pre-2026 research in consumer neuroscience suggests that implicit emotional processes are key predictors of downstream outcomes like electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM). A rigorous, multi-method validation of these implicit measures in an ecologically valid social media context is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: Implicit psychophysiological measures (EEG frontal alpha asymmetry as an index of approach motivation, and eye-tracking pupil dilation as an index of cognitive/emotional arousal) will predict intentions to generate eWOM more accurately than traditional self-reported engagement metrics, and this relationship will be moderated by individual differences in extraversion.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled calibration experiment where participants (N=120) interact with a simulated social media feed containing varied brand page posts. Concurrently, we will collect EEG data (using a 32-channel system) to derive frontal alpha asymmetry (FAA) scores and eye-tracking data (using a Tobii Pro Fusion) to measure pupil dilation and fixation duration. Self-reported engagement and eWOM intention will be captured via surveys. Data analysis will employ partial least squares structural equation modeling (PLS-SEM), a method well-established in pre-2026 consumer research for modeling complex relationships with latent variables, to test the predictive validity of the psychophysiological indices against the survey-based measures, incorporating extraversion as a moderator.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, multi-method framework for measuring true consumer engagement by calibrating implicit neural and ocular responses against traditional metrics. It will advance the methodological rigor called for in consumer research by offering a neuroscientifically-grounded 'gold standard' for engagement, directly informing more effective content strategies for brands. Furthermore, it will extend Sarstedt's prior work on calibration and extraversion by grounding these constructs in objective physiological data.
proposals.novelty: While psychophysiology is used in marketing, its application as a calibration tool for validating social media engagement metrics—specifically integrating EEG and eye-tracking to dissect the motivational and arousal components of engagement—is novel. The explicit test of extraversion as a moderator of these psychophysiological-eWOM links bridges social psychology theory with consumer neuroscience in a new way.

proposals.title: Mapping the Social Architecture of Influence: A Network Analysis of eWOM Diffusion by Extraversion Trait
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Sarstedt's own work, establishes that extraversion influences the generation of electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM) on brand pages. However, the critical question of how eWOM from different personality types subsequently spreads through online social networks remains unexplored. Understanding this diffusion architecture is vital for marketers to identify true influencers beyond mere content generators and to predict the reach and impact of brand-related conversations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that eWOM content originating from highly extraverted individuals will diffuse through networks characterized by higher structural virality (less tree-like, more broadcast) and greater reach, while content from introverts will propagate through stronger, more clustered ties, resulting in deeper but narrower engagement within cohesive subgroups.
proposals.method: We will collect longitudinal data from a social media platform (e.g., Twitter/X or a branded community), using API access to trace retweet/share cascades of brand-related posts. User extraversion will be inferred via a validated linguistic analysis of historical posts using LIWC or a similar pre-2026 dictionary. Social network analysis will be conducted using igraph or NetworkX, applying metrics like cascade size, depth, structural virality, and modularity to map diffusion patterns. Statistical validation will employ multi-group comparison techniques and regression models to test for significant differences in network structures.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond correlating personality with eWOM generation to explain its differential propagation, offering a network-based theory of personality-driven influence. It will provide managers with a methodological framework to distinguish between creators of content and catalysts of diffusion, enabling more efficient targeting of marketing resources in social media campaigns.
proposals.novelty: While extraversion's role in generating eWOM is known, its specific impact on the topological structure of information diffusion networks is a novel investigation. This integrates psychographic segmentation with computational social science methods to reveal the 'social architecture' of influence, a significant advance over static engagement metrics.

proposals.title: Capturing the Dynamic Path to eWOM: An Experience Sampling Study of Momentary Engagement and Sharing Intentions on Social Media
proposals.motivation: Current consumer research on electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM) often relies on retrospective surveys or mined user-generated content, which are prone to recall bias and fail to capture the dynamic, in-the-moment psychological states that precede sharing. Understanding these momentary antecedents is crucial for brands to effectively stimulate organic advocacy. This study addresses this gap by leveraging Experience Sampling Methodology (ESM) to capture real-time data on engagement and eWOM intentions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that momentary states of cognitive and affective brand page engagement, measured in real-time, are significant proximal predictors of immediate eWOM intentions, and that this relationship is moderated by individual-level traits such as extraversion.
proposals.method: We will implement a smartphone-based ESM study where participants are prompted multiple times daily over one week via a dedicated app. Each prompt will measure momentary engagement (using validated multi-item scales for cognitive absorption and affective valence) and eWOM intention. The design will include event-contingent sampling triggered by participants' visits to brand social media pages. Data analysis will employ multilevel structural equation modeling (MSEM) to separate within-person (state) and between-person (trait) effects, and latent growth curve modeling to examine trajectories of engagement over time.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, fine-grained understanding of the psychological micro-foundations of eWOM generation, moving beyond static correlations to causal-like temporal sequences. It will offer methodological advancement by demonstrating the value of ESM for consumer neuroscience and marketing science, and deliver actionable insights for brands on how to design real-time social media interactions to foster advocacy.
proposals.novelty: While ESM is established in psychology, its application to model the dynamic, within-person precursors of eWOM in a naturalistic social media environment is novel. This approach directly addresses the limitations of cross-sectional and mined data highlighted in Sarstedt's prior work on calibration and mindset metrics.

proposals.title: Personality-Driven Engagement: A Conjoint-Based Framework for Optimizing Social Media Brand Page Features
proposals.motivation: Brands invest heavily in social media engagement features (e.g., polls, live videos, user-generated content campaigns), but their effectiveness is inconsistent. Pre-2026 research, including Sarstedt's work on extraversion and eWOM, suggests personality traits significantly moderate engagement outcomes. However, a systematic method to design optimal feature combinations for distinct personality segments is lacking, leading to inefficient resource allocation and suboptimal consumer experiences.
proposals.hypothesis: The perceived utility and choice likelihood of social media brand page engagement features are contingent on user personality profiles, such that optimal feature bundles can be derived for specific segments (e.g., high vs. low extraversion) using adapted conjoint analysis.
proposals.method: We will employ a choice-based conjoint (CBC) experiment where participants evaluate profiles of a social media brand page defined by attributes like feature type (interactive, informative, co-creative), reward mechanism, and social visibility. Following Sarstedt's calibration approach, we will validate the personality segmentation (using the Big Five Inventory) with behavioral data from a simulated social media environment. Hierarchical Bayes (HB) estimation will be used to derive individual-level part-worth utilities, which will then be clustered to identify personality-based segments. Optimal feature bundles for each segment will be identified via market simulations using Sawtooth Software.
proposals.contribution: This research contributes a novel methodological framework that bridges experimental design (conjoint analysis) and psychographic segmentation for strategic social media design. It provides managers with a data-driven tool for personality-targeted engagement strategy, moving beyond one-size-fits-all approaches. Theoretically, it advances understanding of the contingent value of social media affordances.
proposals.novelty: While conjoint analysis is established in product design, its adaptation to optimize intangible social media engagement features for psychological segments is novel. The integration of validated personality metrics with choice modeling for digital strategy design represents a significant advance beyond correlational studies of personality and engagement.

proposals.title: Beyond the Mainstream: Modeling Parasocial Relationship Drivers and eWOM in Micro-Influencer Brand Communities
proposals.motivation: Prior research on social media brand pages and electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM) has predominantly focused on large-scale, corporate-managed communities. However, the marketing landscape (pre-2026) is increasingly dominated by micro-influencers, whose small, niche brand pages may foster qualitatively different consumer-brand dynamics. Understanding the specific mechanisms of parasocial relationship formation and eWOM generation in these intimate, low-scale settings is a critical gap with significant implications for influencer marketing and brand community management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in micro-influencer brand communities, the drivers of parasocial relationships (PSRs) and subsequent eWOM differ significantly from those in large-scale brand communities, with factors like perceived authenticity and interactive reciprocity playing a disproportionately stronger role than content volume or production quality.
proposals.method: We will apply and extend the structural equation model from Sarstedt's work on extraversion and eWOM to a new context. Data will be mined via APIs from selected micro-influencer Instagram or TikTok brand pages (follower count 1K-50K). User-generated content (comments, replies) and engagement metrics will be analyzed using natural language processing (specifically, sentiment analysis via VADER and LIWC dictionaries, and topic modeling via Latent Dirichlet Allocation) to operationalize latent constructs like perceived authenticity and reciprocity. A calibration experiment, following Sarstedt's validation approach, will be designed to validate these mined metrics against self-reported survey data from community members.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, context-specific model for PSRs and eWOM in micro-influencer communities, offering actionable insights for influencer selection and community management. It will extend measurement validation techniques (calibration experiments) to novel, mined constructs derived from small-scale UGC, contributing to methodological rigor in digital consumer research.
proposals.novelty: This research shifts the analytical lens from large, corporate brand pages to the understudied domain of micro-influencer communities, explicitly testing whether established models of social media engagement scale down or require fundamental re-specification due to altered social dynamics.

proposals.title: Inferring Consumer Engagement and Personality from Electronic Word-of-Mouth: An Inverse Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: Current consumer research primarily models engagement and personality traits as antecedents of eWOM. However, for brands and platforms, observed eWOM is often the only available data. The inverse problem—using eWOM to infer latent user states—remains underexplored, limiting the ability to personalize interactions and predict future behavior from existing digital footprints.
proposals.hypothesis: The linguistic, temporal, and social network features of a user's eWOM can be used to accurately infer their underlying engagement patterns (e.g., frequency, valence, platform-specificity) and core personality traits, particularly extraversion, which is strongly linked to social media behavior.
proposals.method: We will collect a large-scale dataset of user-generated content (UGC) from social media brand pages, linking it to self-reported survey data on engagement and personality (e.g., BFI-10). Using natural language processing (NLP), we will extract linguistic features (sentiment, LIWC categories, topic modeling) and behavioral features (posting frequency, response time). We will then train and compare several machine learning models, including regularized regression (LASSO), random forests, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), to solve this inverse prediction problem, validating model performance via calibration experiments as proposed in Sarstedt's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, data-driven framework for user profiling based solely on observable eWOM, moving beyond traditional forward causal models. It will offer marketers a practical tool for segmenting and targeting consumers more effectively and contribute methodologically by applying inverse problem-solving and calibration validation to consumer psychology.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses traits to predict eWOM, this study uniquely inverts the causal direction. It synthesizes Sarstedt's expertise in measurement validation (calibration experiments), mining UGC, and the role of extraversion in eWOM into a single, novel predictive modeling framework.

proposals.title: Beyond the Brand: Testing the Generalizability of eWOM Drivers from Commercial Brand Pages to Non-Commercial Social Media Pages
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM) and social media engagement is heavily skewed toward commercial brand pages, leaving a significant gap in understanding user behavior on non-commercial pages for causes, NGOs, or public figures. These non-commercial entities are crucial for social discourse and mobilization, yet it is unclear if established consumer behavior models, like the role of extraversion in driving eWOM, apply in these contexts where motivations are less transactional.
proposals.hypothesis: The positive relationship between user extraversion and eWOM generation, mediated by social media page engagement, is significantly weaker for non-commercial pages (e.g., for social causes or public figures) than for commercial brand pages, due to differing primary user motivations (e.g., altruism vs. self-enhancement).
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-platform survey study, recruiting users who actively follow both commercial brand and non-commercial cause/public figure pages. Using established scales, we will measure extraversion, page engagement, and eWOM intentions. Following Sarstedt's calibration experiments logic, we will employ structural equation modeling (SEM) with multi-group analysis to test for metric invariance and compare path coefficients (extraversion -> engagement -> eWOM) across page types. We will supplement this with a text-mining component, applying LIWC or a pre-trained BERT model to a sample of user-generated comments to validate the motivational constructs inferred from the survey.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first empirical test of a core consumer psychology model's boundary conditions in the non-commercial social media sphere. It will offer actionable insights for managers of cause-related pages on how to effectively foster engagement and advocacy. The study will also advance methodological discourse on measurement validation when scaling constructs across fundamentally different contexts.
proposals.novelty: While the extraversion-engagement-eWOM link is established for brands, applying and testing this model in the non-commercial domain is novel. The direct, calibrated comparison of parameter estimates across commercial and non-commercial contexts using a multi-method approach (survey SEM and text mining) moves beyond simple context replication to theory refinement.

proposals.title: Beyond Additivity: A Copula-Based Framework for Modeling Interacting Sources of Measurement Uncertainty in Consumer Research
proposals.motivation: Marketing measurement (e.g., of brand sentiment, consumer mindset) is plagued by multiple, often correlated, uncertainty sources like method bias, sampling error, and model misspecification. Pre-2026 validation practices, including multi-method approaches and calibration experiments, often treat these uncertainties as separable and additive, a simplification that can misrepresent total measurement error and lead to invalid inferences. This is critical for consumer research relying on user-generated content and sensory data, where error sources are inherently intertwined.
proposals.hypothesis: The interactive, non-additive nature of key uncertainty sources (e.g., common method variance interacting with algorithmic bias in text mining) can be formally modeled, leading to more accurate uncertainty quantification and improved validity of consumer constructs compared to traditional additive-error models.
proposals.method: We propose a copula-based structural equation modeling (SEM) framework to model dependence between latent error terms representing different uncertainty sources. Using data from calibration experiments and mined user-generated content, we will specify and estimate a model where, for instance, the error distribution of a text-mined sentiment score (algorithmic uncertainty) is linked via a copula function to the error of a self-reported scale (method uncertainty). Estimation will employ a Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) approach, implemented in Stan, to fit the complex likelihood. Model comparison will use Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion (WAIC) against traditional additive-error SEM.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a formal, generalizable methodology for modeling non-separable uncertainties, advancing measurement theory beyond multi-method triangulation. It offers applied researchers a practical framework for more rigorous validation, particularly for novel metrics derived from big data and social media, thereby increasing the scientific capital of consumer research.
proposals.novelty: While copulas are used in finance for risk modeling, their application to model the *interaction* of psychometric and data-scientific uncertainty sources in marketing is novel. This moves beyond diagnosing separable biases to explicitly modeling their joint stochastic structure.

proposals.title: From Statistical to Managerial Significance: A Formal Framework for Defining the Region of Practical Equivalence (ROPE) in Marketing Research
proposals.motivation: The Bayesian ROPE framework is gaining traction for testing null effects, but its application in marketing lacks methodological rigor. Current practice relies on ad-hoc or arbitrary thresholds, which undermines the validity of conclusions about the practical insignificance of effects (e.g., a brand lift or price elasticity). This creates a critical gap between statistical inference and managerial decision-making, where defining what constitutes a 'trivial' effect is context-dependent and consequential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, multi-method framework integrating consumer mindset metrics, managerial input, and financial data can generate robust, context-specific ROPEs that are more valid and actionable than current arbitrary standards.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-study calibration approach. First, we will mine user-generated content and survey data across diverse contexts (e.g., brand engagement, sensory marketing) to establish empirical distributions of effect sizes. Second, we will conduct choice-based conjoint experiments with embedded financial anchors to calibrate ROPEs to monetary indifference points. Third, we will validate these ROPEs using predictive validation techniques, assessing their performance in forecasting managerial decisions versus arbitrary benchmarks (e.g., half a Cohen's d).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first formal, empirically grounded guidelines for defining ROPEs in consumer research, moving the field beyond arbitrary thresholds. It will deliver a validated toolkit for researchers and enhance the credibility and actionability of Bayesian equivalence testing for practitioners by linking statistical intervals to business relevance.
proposals.novelty: While ROPE is known, a systematic methodology for its definition in applied social science is absent. This work innovates by integrating consumer science data (mindset metrics, sensory capital) with behavioral economics calibration to formalize what has been a subjective judgment.

proposals.title: Reassessing Replicability in Marketing: A Metrological Uncertainty Framework for Interpreting Effect Size Distributions
proposals.motivation: The replication crisis has highlighted the fragility of many findings in consumer and marketing research, but the binary classification of studies as 'successes' or 'failures' is overly simplistic. Pre-2026, the field lacks a formal framework to quantify and interpret the inherent uncertainty in replicated effect estimates, moving beyond statistical significance. This limits our understanding of whether a non-replication indicates a false original finding, contextual moderators, or measurement variability.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a metrological uncertainty framework to marketing replication studies will reveal that many apparent 'failures' are consistent with the original effect when the combined standard uncertainty of both studies is formally considered, and that the dispersion of replication estimates can be systematically modeled by study-level covariates.
proposals.method: We will apply the metrological framework from Sarstedt's (pre-2026) calibration experiments paper, treating each original and replication study as a measurement system. For a curated set of marketing replications (e.g., from the 'Many Labs' projects or JMR replication series), we will compute the combined standard uncertainty for each effect size pair. We will then use Bayesian tolerance intervals to assess compatibility. Furthermore, we will employ meta-analytic random forests (a machine learning method established pre-2026) on study features (e.g., sample source, sensory vs. survey design, brand equity) to predict heterogeneity in replication effect sizes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a quantitative, uncertainty-aware tool for the field to evaluate replication outcomes, reducing dichotomous thinking. It will generate a diagnostic typology for replication discrepancies (e.g., measurement instability vs. contextual shift). The findings will inform guidelines for designing more robust, replicable consumer studies by explicitly accounting for key sources of uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: While multi-method validation exists, applying a formal metrological (physical science) uncertainty framework to replication meta-science in marketing is novel. The integration of this framework with machine learning to model heterogeneity moves beyond traditional meta-regression by capturing complex, non-linear interactions between study features.

proposals.title: A Metrological Framework for Marketing: Quantifying and Reporting Measurement Uncertainty in Common Consumer Scales
proposals.motivation: Marketing research relies heavily on standardized scales (e.g., for brand attitude, engagement, personality) to test theories and inform decisions. However, the field lacks a formal, quantitative practice for reporting the inherent imprecision of these measurements, unlike fields such as physics or chemistry. This omission obscures the reliability of findings and hinders the cumulative progress of consumer science, as effect sizes and model parameters are interpreted without an understanding of their associated measurement error bands.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying metrological principles—specifically, the Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement (GUM) framework—to common marketing scales will reveal significant and variable levels of measurement uncertainty that are currently unreported. This uncertainty systematically affects the interpretation of statistical results and model comparisons.
proposals.method: Building on Sarstedt's calibration experiments approach, we will conduct a multi-study design. First, we will identify 10-15 widely used multi-item scales from major marketing journals. For each, we will collect large-scale, repeated-measures data (N > 500 per scale) using high-frequency online panels. We will employ a Bayesian structural equation modeling (BSEM) framework to decompose variance into trait, method, and random error components. This model will be integrated with a Monte Carlo simulation to propagate these variance estimates through common analytical workflows (e.g., regression, mediation analysis), generating a combined standard uncertainty for key model parameters (e.g., path coefficients).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first systematic, empirically derived estimates of measurement uncertainty for foundational marketing constructs. It will offer a practical template for reporting uncertainty intervals alongside point estimates, enhancing the interpretability and robustness of empirical findings. Ultimately, it aims to elevate measurement standards in consumer research by importing rigorous metrological practices from the physical sciences.
proposals.novelty: While multi-method validation and calibration experiments exist, directly translating the formal GUM framework—a cornerstone of metrology—to assess and propagate uncertainty in latent psychological constructs is novel. This moves beyond assessing scale quality (e.g., via reliability) to quantifying how measurement imprecision cascades through and distorts substantive conclusions.

proposals.title: Developing and Validating an Open-Source Software Tool for Informative Null Hypothesis Analysis in Consumer Research
proposals.motivation: Consumer research, particularly in marketing and social sciences, faces a replication crisis and a publication bias favoring statistically significant results. Pre-2026 literature highlights the need to move beyond simple 'null hypothesis significance testing' to meaningfully evaluate non-significant findings. A formal inferential framework for null results is essential for robust science, but its adoption is hindered by a lack of accessible, standardized software tools.
proposals.hypothesis: The development and dissemination of a dedicated, user-friendly software package implementing a three-step inferential framework (testing against nil, equivalence, and smallest effect size of interest) will significantly improve the rigor and interpretative depth of null result analyses in consumer studies.
proposals.method: We will develop a Python/R package implementing the three-step framework (Lakens et al., 2018). The core architecture will integrate: 1) Standard null hypothesis significance tests, 2) Two One-Sided Tests (TOST) for equivalence, and 3) Bayesian and frequentist methods for assessing the smallest effect size of interest (SESOI). The software will feature a GUI for applied researchers and an API for integration into existing analysis pipelines (e.g., with SEM software like SmartPLS). Validation will involve calibration experiments and applying the tool to re-analyze datasets from published studies with null results.
proposals.contribution: This project provides the first comprehensive, open-source software dedicated to informative null analysis for consumer researchers. It directly addresses methodological calls for better null result evaluation, enhancing research validity. By lowering the technical barrier, it empowers researchers to confidently publish and interpret non-significant findings, mitigating publication bias.
proposals.novelty: While the statistical framework exists, no dedicated, consumer-research-focused software package integrates all three inferential steps with a user-friendly interface. The novelty lies in operationalizing this framework into a practical tool, validated specifically for consumer research contexts like brand engagement and user-generated content analysis.

proposals.title: Decomposing Measurement Error in Consumer Mindset Metrics: A Gage R&R Framework for Marketing Science
proposals.motivation: In consumer research, the validity of latent constructs (e.g., brand engagement, perceived quality) is frequently assessed via multi-method approaches or calibration experiments, yet these methods often fail to systematically partition variance into components attributable to the measurement instrument (e.g., survey scale design, sensory test protocol) versus the inherent variability among respondents. This obscures the fundamental reliability of our measurement systems, leading to potential misinterpretation of effect sizes and inefficient study designs. Borrowing a proven framework from manufacturing quality control can provide a rigorous, quantitative diagnosis of measurement error sources in marketing data.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Gage Repeatability and Reproducibility (R&R) analysis to consumer response data will reveal that a significant portion of the total variance in commonly used mindset metrics is attributable to instrument-related factors (repeatability and reproducibility), rather than true variation between respondents or consumer segments.
proposals.method: We will adapt the standard crossed-design ANOVA-based Gage R&R methodology. Specific studies will involve repeated measurements where multiple respondents (parts) evaluate multiple stimuli (e.g., brands, products) using a given instrument (e.g., a multi-item scale, a sensory panel protocol) administered by different researchers or at different times (operators). The variance components for part (respondent), operator (instrument administrator/time), and their interaction will be estimated. We will apply this to data from structured surveys and controlled sensory tests, calculating standard metrics like %Study Variation and Number of Distinct Categories to assess measurement system adequacy for marketing research purposes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide marketing scientists with a standardized, quantitative framework to audit the reliability of their measurement instruments, moving beyond construct validation to system validation. It will yield concrete guidelines for improving scale design and data collection protocols by identifying the largest sources of measurement noise. The study will bridge methodological rigor between engineering and social science, enhancing the credibility of empirical consumer research.
proposals.novelty: While Gage R&R is a cornerstone of quality engineering, its formal application to partition variance in perceptual and attitudinal marketing data is novel. It extends Sarstedt's prior work on calibration experiments by offering a specific, variance-component-based alternative for diagnosing measurement systems, rather than solely validating constructs.

proposals.title: Beyond Statistical Significance: An Informative Null Framework for Evaluating Micro-Interventions in Digital Marketing
proposals.motivation: Digital marketing increasingly relies on high-frequency A/B testing of micro-interventions (e.g., minor UI changes, micro-copy variations). Pre-2026, the dominant paradigm focuses on detecting statistically significant effects, often dismissing small, precisely estimated null results as failures. This leads to wasted data and missed opportunities to learn what *doesn't* work. The field lacks a principled framework to extract value from these highly precise null findings.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the informative null hypothesis framework to digital marketing A/B tests will allow researchers to formally accept the null hypothesis of no meaningful effect for micro-interventions, thereby accumulating robust knowledge about ineffective tactics and reducing false-positive exploration.
proposals.method: We will analyze a large corpus of historical A/B test data from e-commerce and social media brand pages, focusing on tests with high precision (narrow confidence intervals) but non-significant point estimates. Using the Two One-Sided Tests (TOST) procedure for equivalence testing, we will define a smallest effect size of practical interest (SESOI) based on managerial input and historical conversion data. Bayesian methods (e.g., Bayes factors with informed priors) will be employed to quantify evidence for the null. The technical implementation will involve scripting in R (using packages like 'TOSTER' and 'BayesFactor') to automate the re-evaluation of past 'failed' experiments.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a methodological bridge between statistical rigor and managerial actionability for null results. It contributes to measurement validation in consumer research by offering a formal calibration for concluding ineffectiveness. The output will be a decision heuristic for marketers to distinguish true nulls from underpowered tests, improving the efficiency of digital experimentation.
proposals.novelty: While equivalence testing is known in clinical trials, its application to consumer-focused A/B testing for validating the *absence* of meaningful effects is novel. This extends Sarstedt's work on calibration experiments and mindset metrics by providing a formal framework for learning from negative results in user-generated digital contexts.

proposals.title: Beyond the Mean Effect: A Metrological Framework for Propagating Measurement Uncertainty in Consumer Research Meta-Analyses
proposals.motivation: Meta-analyses in consumer and marketing research synthesize effect sizes but typically treat each study's point estimate as a precise, error-free input. This ignores the fundamental metrological uncertainty inherent in the measurement instruments (e.g., scales, sensory tests, UGC-derived metrics) used in the primary studies. Consequently, traditional meta-analytic confidence intervals may be artificially narrow, overstating the precision of the synthesized evidence and potentially leading to misguided theoretical conclusions and managerial recommendations.
proposals.hypothesis: Formally incorporating measurement uncertainty from primary studies into meta-analytic models will yield more accurate and conservative estimates of true effect sizes and their credible intervals, significantly altering inferences in domains where measurement validity is a known concern (e.g., brand engagement, consumer mindset metrics).
proposals.method: We will extend random-effects meta-analysis by treating each study's observed effect size as a distribution informed by its reported standard error AND an estimate of its measurement uncertainty. For primary studies reporting calibration experiment results (per Sarstedt et al.), we will use their validity coefficients to quantify this uncertainty. For others, we will employ Bayesian hierarchical models to impute plausible uncertainty distributions based on study characteristics (e.g., scale type, context). The synthesis will be performed using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling in Stan, explicitly propagating these uncertainty distributions through the meta-analytic model to derive a posterior distribution for the population effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal methodology for integrating metrological principles into evidence synthesis in consumer research, moving the field toward more statistically honest meta-analyses. It will offer applied researchers a practical framework to assess how measurement quality moderates cumulative knowledge, thereby strengthening the foundation for theory testing and data-driven decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While meta-analysis is well-established, and Sarstedt's recent work advocates for calibration in primary studies, no pre-2026 method exists to systematically propagate this calibration-based measurement uncertainty into the meta-analytic process itself, creating a critical gap between primary and secondary research methodologies.

proposals.title: Global Algorithms, Local People: A Multi-Group SEM of Digital and AI-Driven Heritage Value Creation
proposals.motivation: The digitization of cultural heritage and the rise of AI-driven curation (e.g., algorithmic recommendations on platforms like Google Arts & Culture) are fundamentally altering how heritage value is created and monetized. Pre-2026 research on heritage value creation remains fragmented, often focusing on single contexts or traditional human capital, lacking a unified model to test whether digital pathways are universally applicable while human factors remain context-dependent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structural paths from digital platform engagement and AI-driven curation to perceived heritage value and economic outcomes (e.g., willingness-to-pay, visit intention) are global and stable across different heritage clusters, whereas the influence of traditional human capital (e.g., curator expertise, community involvement) is cluster-specific and potentially diminished in digitally mature contexts.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-group structural equation modeling (SEM) approach, specifically a multi-cluster structural model. Data will be collected via surveys and mined user-generated content (UGC) from diverse heritage contexts (e.g., UNESCO sites, local museums, digital archives). Using established SEM software (e.g., Mplus, SmartPLS), we will specify a baseline model and then test for measurement invariance. Crucially, we will constrain paths from digital/AI constructs to outcomes to be equal across clusters (global), while freely estimating paths from human capital (cluster-specific), using chi-square difference tests to validate this configuration.
proposals.contribution: This research provides a novel empirical framework to disentangle universal digital drivers from contingent human factors in heritage value creation. It contributes to marketing and consumer research by extending structural modeling to test theory about non-human capital, and offers heritage managers a diagnostic tool to prioritize investments in digital infrastructure versus traditional expertise.
proposals.novelty: The proposal's novelty lies in its explicit hypothesis and methodological design to treat technological pathways as global constants and human capital as local variables within a unified cross-contextual model, moving beyond simple moderation tests or single-context studies prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Causal Archetypes of Heritage Performance: A Data-Driven Discovery of Micro-Heterogeneous Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Heritage site management suffers from a one-size-fits-all approach, where generalized best practices ignore the unique configurations of territorial capital (e.g., sensory appeal, brand equity, community social capital) that drive success. Pre-2026, consumer research in tourism relies heavily on correlational models and pre-defined typologies, failing to uncover the distinct causal pathways through which assets affect outcomes like visitor engagement for specific clusters of sites. This limits actionable diagnostics for managers.
proposals.hypothesis: Heritage sites cluster into distinct archetypes defined not only by shared observable features but by shared underlying causal structures linking their territorial capital to performance outcomes. The most effective managerial interventions are contingent on a site's causal archetype.
proposals.method: We will integrate constraint-based causal discovery (specifically the PC or FCI algorithm) with model-based clustering (e.g., finite mixture modeling). For a panel dataset of individual museums/parks, we will first apply clustering on a broad set of capital assets. Within each resulting cluster, we will run causal discovery algorithms on variables including sensory capital, brand sentiment (mined from user-generated content), social media engagement, and financial sustainability to learn a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) representing the cluster-specific causal mechanism. We will validate the stability of the causal structures using calibration experiment principles.
proposals.contribution: This research provides a novel methodological framework for discovering micro-heterogeneous causal mechanisms in consumer and marketing contexts, moving beyond average effects. For heritage science, it delivers a diagnostic tool that identifies archetype-specific leverage points, enabling managers to tailor strategies based on their site's causal profile rather than generic benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of causal discovery with clustering to define groups by causal structure, rather than just covariates, is novel in consumer research. It bypasses the need for a priori theorized typologies, allowing data-driven causal archetypes to emerge from the integration of territorial capital metrics.

proposals.title: Enhancing Measurement in Consumer Science: A Regularized Calibration Algorithm for High-Dimensional Manifest Variables
proposals.motivation: Consumer research increasingly leverages high-dimensional data from user-generated content, sensory science, and brand tracking, where latent constructs are measured with many indicators. Traditional structural equation modeling (SEM) struggles with such high-dimensional manifest variables, leading to model instability, overfitting, and interpretability challenges. This gap hinders the validation of complex consumer mindset metrics and the robust testing of marketing theories.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a variable selection mechanism within a partial least squares (PLS) path modeling framework will yield more parsimonious, stable, and interpretable measurement models for high-dimensional consumer data without significant loss of predictive validity.
proposals.method: We will extend the PLS-SEM algorithm by embedding a regularization penalty (e.g., Lasso or Elastic Net) within the outer model estimation step to perform simultaneous variable selection and latent score calculation. The algorithm will be implemented in R, building on the 'plspm' or 'semPLS' packages, and validated through Monte Carlo simulations varying dimensionality, sample size, and noise levels. Empirical validation will use a pre-2026 dataset of brand-related user-generated content with many textual indicators per latent brand perception construct.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a practical, scalable tool for consumer researchers to distill high-dimensional data into robust, interpretable latent variables. It directly advances Sarstedt's work on calibration experiments and mindset metrics by offering a principled method for indicator selection, moving beyond ad-hoc scale purification. The method will enhance the rigor of measurement validation in marketing and social science contexts.
proposals.novelty: While regularization is known in statistics, its integration into the iterative PLS-SEM algorithm for automated indicator selection in consumer research is novel. This approach differs from traditional multi-method validation by providing a data-driven, model-internal selection mechanism tailored for high-dimensional, noisy consumer data.

proposals.title: A Field Experiment on the Adoption and Impact of Registered Reports in Consumer and Marketing Research
proposals.motivation: Publication bias and questionable research practices, such as p-hacking and HARKing, have been identified as major threats to the credibility of psychological science, leading to the development of Registered Reports (RRs) as a corrective. While consumer and marketing research faces similar methodological challenges, the RR format remains largely absent from leading business journals. This project addresses the critical need to empirically assess whether this innovative publication model can be successfully implemented and can improve research rigor in our field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured intervention to introduce and promote the Registered Reports format in a leading marketing journal will (a) be adopted by a significant number of research teams and (b) result in submitted manuscripts with stronger methodological designs and lower rates of post-hoc hypothesis adjustment compared to traditionally submitted work.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase field experiment in collaboration with a top-tier marketing journal. First, we will design and implement a formal RR submission track. Second, we will use text-mining algorithms (specifically, a combination of keyword analysis and supervised machine learning with BERT-based classifiers) on the submitted Stage 1 protocols to quantify the clarity of hypotheses, pre-registered analysis plans, and power justifications. Third, we will compare these metrics, as well as final publication rates and result characteristics (e.g., prevalence of null findings), between the RR pipeline and a matched sample of traditional submissions over a two-year period.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence on the feasibility and direct effects of adopting Registered Reports in business research. It will offer journal editors a data-driven framework for implementing RRs and give the scholarly community insights into how this format shapes research practices, potentially leading to more robust and replicable consumer science.
proposals.novelty: While the RR format is known in psychology, its application and systematic evaluation in the context of marketing and consumer research—a field with distinct data sources (e.g., sensory, brand, user-generated content) and commercial pressures—is novel. The project moves beyond advocacy to a rigorous, data-centric test of the model's implementation and impact.

proposals.title: Provenance-Aware Consumer Science: A Computational Framework for Tracking and Validating Data Lineage in Multi-Method Consumer Research
