proposals.motivation: Consumer research increasingly relies on complex, multi-source data (e.g., surveys, sensory metrics, user-generated content), raising critical concerns about data integrity, reproducibility, and validation. Pre-2026, methods like calibration experiments and multi-method approaches address validation but lack a systematic, automated way to document the complete lineage—from collection and transformation to analysis—of research artifacts. This gap undermines confidence in findings and complicates the synthesis of insights from disparate data streams.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a formal data provenance framework, adapted from computer science, will significantly enhance the auditability, reproducibility, and construct validity of consumer research data by providing an immutable, machine-readable record of its origin and processing history.
proposals.method: We will adapt the W3C PROV data model to define core entities (Agent, Entity, Activity) for consumer research workflows. A lightweight software layer will be integrated into common research platforms (e.g., Qualtrics, R/Python pipelines) to automatically capture provenance metadata. For text mining of user-generated content (e.g., using LDA or BERT-based classifiers), the framework will log preprocessing steps, parameter settings, and model versions. Validation will involve calibration experiments where known-manipulated datasets are processed, with the provenance trace used to pinpoint introduced errors.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a novel methodological infrastructure for transparent and verifiable consumer research, directly extending Sarstedt's work on calibration and measurement validation. It offers a concrete tool for researchers to document data lineage, facilitating stronger claims about construct validity and enabling more robust meta-analyses of consumer mindset metrics.
proposals.novelty: While data provenance is established in computer science, its application to the specific validation and multi-method challenges in consumer research is novel. It moves beyond post-hoc validation methods by embedding verifiability directly into the research data pipeline.

proposals.title: Auditing the Consumer Research Corpus: A Metascientific Framework for Assessing Methodological Rigor and Reporting Transparency
proposals.motivation: The replication crisis in psychology and social sciences has highlighted widespread methodological issues, but business and consumer research has not undergone a systematic, large-scale audit of its own practices. Given the field's influence on marketing strategy and public policy, understanding the robustness of its evidence base is critical. Pre-2026, metascience has developed rigorous audit frameworks for other disciplines, yet these have not been formally adapted and applied to the consumer research corpus.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic application of metascientific audit methods will reveal significant heterogeneity in methodological rigor and reporting transparency across the consumer research literature, with practices correlating with journal prestige, article age, and methodological approach (e.g., experimental vs. observational).
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, automated audit of empirical articles from leading consumer research journals (e.g., JCR, JCP, JMR) published 2010-2025. Using natural language processing (specifically, fine-tuned BERT models for scientific text classification) and rule-based text mining, we will extract and code key methodological indicators: statistical power justification, p-value reporting standards, pre-registration mentions, data/ code sharing statements, and conflict of interest disclosures. We will employ regression and network analysis to examine patterns and predictors of rigorous reporting.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative baseline of methodological health in consumer research, identifying specific areas for improvement. It will deliver a validated, automated audit framework that journals and authors can use for self-assessment, promoting a culture of transparency. The findings will inform editorial policies and methodological training within the marketing discipline.
proposals.novelty: While metascientific audits exist in biomedicine and psychology, this is the first to systematically adapt and apply these frameworks to the distinct methodologies and reporting norms of consumer research. It moves beyond single-case replication failures to provide a population-level diagnostic of the field's research integrity.

proposals.title: Opening the Black Box: A Field Experiment on the Impact of Open Peer Review on Review Quality, Bias, and Author Perception in Consumer Research
proposals.motivation: The traditional single-blind peer review system in business and consumer research journals is criticized for potential biases, lack of transparency, and inconsistent quality. Pre-2026, open peer review (OPR) models with open identities and open reports have gained traction in physics (e.g., journals like Nature Communications, Copernicus journals) and other sciences, but their adoption and effects in social science fields like marketing remain largely unexplored. This creates a significant research-practice gap regarding how OPR might influence the validity and social dynamics of knowledge production in our field.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing an open peer review (OPR) system in a consumer research journal context will (a) increase the perceived quality and constructiveness of reviews, (b) reduce demographic and affiliation-based biases in reviewer recommendations, and (c) lead to more positive author perceptions of fairness compared to traditional single-blind review.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized field experiment in collaboration with a journal editor. Manuscripts will be randomly assigned to either a traditional single-blind condition or an OPR condition (open identities, open published reports). We will measure outcomes using: (1) multi-method calibration experiments to validate scales for perceived review quality and bias; (2) automated text mining of review content using LIWC and sentiment analysis to assess constructiveness; (3) analysis of reviewer recommendations (accept/reject) controlling for author gender, institution prestige, and methodological approach (e.g., sensory vs. data science); and (4) post-decision surveys of authors measuring perceived fairness and satisfaction.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical, causal evidence on the effects of OPR in a business research context, directly informing journal policy debates. It will extend methodological validation techniques (calibration experiments) to the novel domain of evaluating scientific processes. The findings will contribute to the science of science by understanding how transparency reforms impact social and consumer research specifically.
proposals.novelty: While OPR is discussed in abstract terms, this proposal applies a rigorous, multi-method experimental design—grounded in consumer research methodology—to test its effects empirically within the field's own ecosystem, moving beyond physics-based assumptions to account for the unique social and brand capital dynamics of business academia.

proposals.title: Lightweight Open Science for Consumer-Centric Case Studies: A Framework for Transparent and Actionable Qualitative Research
proposals.motivation: Open science practices are predominantly designed for large-scale, quantitative studies, creating a barrier for qualitative and single-case research in consumer and marketing science. This limits the transparency, credibility, and cumulative knowledge-building potential of rich, context-specific investigations into consumer behavior, brand phenomena, and social media engagement. A scalable, practical framework is needed to make open science accessible for the in-depth, nuanced studies that are central to the field.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, 'minimum viable' open science framework, tailored to the logistical and ethical constraints of qualitative case study research, will significantly increase the perceived feasibility and adoption of transparent practices among consumer researchers without compromising methodological rigor.
proposals.method: We will develop and validate the framework through a multi-phase design science approach. First, we will conduct a systematic review of pre-2026 qualitative open science attempts and synthesize key barriers. Second, we will iteratively co-design a lightweight protocol with consumer researchers, focusing on scalable practices for pre-registering qualitative analysis plans, creating anonymized 'thick description' repositories, and using version-controlled qualitative data analysis software (e.g., NVivo, ATLAS.ti) for audit trails. Third, we will test the framework's utility and usability via calibration experiments with researchers replicating analysis on shared case data from user-generated content (e.g., brand social media pages).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first dedicated, actionable framework for applying open science principles to consumer-focused qualitative and case study research. It will advance methodological rigor in the field by offering concrete tools for transparency, thereby enhancing the credibility and cumulative value of in-depth consumer insights. The output will be a practical guideline paper and a template for 'open case study' pre-registration.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond simply advocating for open science in qualitative work to providing a concrete, calibrated solution that addresses the specific practical and ethical challenges (e.g., data richness vs. anonymity) faced by consumer researchers, bridging the gap between open science ideals and grounded, context-sensitive research practice.

proposals.title: Building Trust, Scaling Impact: A Framework for Industry-Wide Data Collaboratives in Consumer Sustainability Research
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the urgent need to understand and promote sustainable consumer behavior is hampered by data fragmentation and proprietary silos. Individual firms possess limited, non-representative data, while researchers lack access to large-scale, cross-industry behavioral data, stifling robust insights. This proposal addresses this critical gap by operationalizing the concept of data collaboratives to create a novel, scalable infrastructure for open business research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a governance and technical framework based on federated learning and differential privacy can overcome competitive and privacy barriers, enabling the formation of large-scale, cross-industry data collaboratives. Furthermore, we hypothesize that models trained on such collaboratively pooled data will yield significantly more generalizable and actionable insights into drivers of sustainable consumer behavior than models trained on single-firm datasets.
proposals.method: The project will develop a technical prototype for a data collaborative. We will employ a federated learning architecture (e.g., using frameworks like TensorFlow Federated or PySyft) where participant firms train local models on their proprietary data, sharing only model parameter updates, not raw data. To ensure privacy, we will integrate differential privacy mechanisms into the aggregation process. The consortium's focus will be curated, multi-modal data (transactional, social media engagement, sensory brand descriptors) related to sustainable product choices. Validation will involve calibration experiments, comparing model performance on held-out data against models trained on single-source and synthetically pooled data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical blueprint for creating large-scale data collaboratives, moving the concept from theory to implementation. It will contribute a novel, privacy-preserving dataset for sustainability research, enabling tests of consumer theories at an unprecedented industry scale. Methodologically, it advances measurement validation in federated contexts, addressing a key challenge for the future of consumer data science.
proposals.novelty: While data collaboratives are discussed conceptually, a practical, scalable implementation for competitive consumer industries using advanced privacy-preserving federated learning is novel. It uniquely bridges Sarstedt's expertise in measurement validation, multi-method research, and mining user-generated content with the logistical challenge of cross-firm data cooperation.

proposals.title: Validating Consumer Insights in the Black Box: A Calibration Framework for Proprietary Data and Models
proposals.motivation: The replication crisis and push for open science in consumer research conflict with the reality of proprietary data and models held by firms, where full disclosure is impossible due to confidentiality and competitive advantage. This creates a credibility gap for findings derived from such 'closed' sources, hindering scientific progress and practical application. A methodological framework is needed to validate insights without exposing raw data or core algorithms.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured calibration framework, using strategically released partial data and model outputs, can provide sufficient statistical evidence to validate the reliability and generalizability of findings from proprietary consumer data and black-box models.
proposals.method: We propose extending Sarstedt's calibration experiment logic to the proprietary context. For a firm's black-box model (e.g., a deep learning model for mining user-generated content), we design a tiered validation protocol. This involves the firm generating and releasing: (1) synthetic or heavily perturbed datasets that preserve key covariance structures, (2) aggregated posterior predictive checks for key outcomes, and (3) results from 'adversarial' calibration experiments where known effects (e.g., extraversion's role in eWOM) are tested within the proprietary system. Validation employs permutation tests and consistency analysis across these tiers.
proposals.contribution: This provides a practical, methodological bridge between open science ideals and corporate data constraints, increasing the credibility of proprietary research. It offers firms a blueprint for demonstrating scientific rigor, and gives the academic community tools to critically assess closed studies, advancing measurement validation in applied consumer science.
proposals.novelty: While multi-method validation exists, applying a calibration philosophy to create a verifiable 'audit trail' for completely hidden data and models is novel. It inverts the typical open science process, focusing on what minimal, safe information must be revealed to establish trust.

proposals.title: The Cost of Opacity: Quantifying Economic and Credibility Losses of Non-Open Research Practices in Consumer Science
proposals.motivation: The open science movement has highlighted the benefits of transparency, yet traditional, opaque research practices (e.g., undisclosed flexibility in analysis, unreported measures, non-public data) remain prevalent in consumer and marketing research. The field lacks a concrete, empirical quantification of the costs these practices impose, both in terms of direct economic waste from non-replicable findings and the erosion of public and scientific credibility. This gap hinders a compelling, evidence-based argument for change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that research projects employing traditional, non-open practices incur significant, measurable economic costs (e.g., wasted follow-up research, failed product development) and suffer greater losses in perceived credibility among both academic peers and informed consumers compared to projects adhering to open science principles.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-method calibration study. First, we will mine user-generated content (e.g., social media, forums) using NLP sentiment and topic modeling to construct a 'credibility perception' metric for research topics known for replication failures. Second, we will design a series of controlled, multi-group experiments where participants (academics, practitioners) evaluate research vignettes that systematically vary in openness (e.g., open data vs. no data, pre-registered vs. flexible analysis). We will measure willingness-to-fund (economic proxy) and trust ratings. Finally, we will use structural equation modeling to quantify the relationships between opacity, credibility loss, and economic intent.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first calibrated, empirical estimates of the tangible costs of non-open research in consumer science, moving the debate from philosophical principles to economic and reputational impact. It will extend calibration experiment methodology to meta-scientific questions and offer actionable metrics for journals, institutions, and funding bodies to advocate for transparency.
proposals.novelty: While the value of open science is advocated, the direct quantification of the costs of *not* being open is novel. The approach uniquely combines mined mindset metrics from user-generated content with controlled calibration experiments to translate credibility perceptions into economic terms, a non-obvious integration given existing work.

proposals.title: Bridging the Implementation Gap: An Open Science Framework for Validating and Translating Consumer Science into Managerial Practice
proposals.motivation: A persistent research-practice gap exists in marketing and consumer research, where academic findings often fail to translate into reliable, actionable managerial tools. Pre-2026, open science principles like pre-registration and data sharing have primarily focused on improving internal research validity, but their application stops at publication. This leaves a critical void in ensuring that validated scales and models maintain their psychometric properties and predictive power when operationalized in real-world business contexts, such as brand monitoring dashboards or customer feedback systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a structured, open science translation protocol—encompassing pre-registered calibration experiments, robustness checks, and implementation audits—to consumer mindset metrics will significantly increase the external validity and practical utility of these metrics when deployed in managerial decision-support systems.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-study, mixed-methods framework. First, we will select established consumer mindset constructs (e.g., brand engagement, perceived value) and their validated scales. Using a pre-registered calibration experiment (extending Sarstedt's prior work), we will test scale robustness across heterogeneous consumer samples and simulated noisy, real-world data conditions. Second, we will partner with firms to implement these scales in live social media listening or customer feedback tools. We will employ algorithmic auditing techniques, comparing the performance of the deployed metric (e.g., using NLP for user-generated content mining) against the gold-standard survey measure, tracking divergence over time and across contexts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, actionable framework for the responsible translation of academic research into practice, moving beyond validation for publication to validation for implementation. It will contribute a new 'implementation validity' criterion to the measurement literature and offer managers a verified protocol for adopting academic insights, thereby enhancing evidence-based decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While open science addresses research credibility, and Sarstedt's calibration experiments address measurement validity, this project uniquely extends these principles into the post-publication, practical application phase, creating a continuous validation pipeline from lab to field.

proposals.title: Beyond the Average Effect: A Methodological Benchmarking Study of Integrative SEM-Clustering to Uncover Heterogeneous Tourism Impact Pathways
proposals.motivation: Tourism's complex impacts on heritage value and resident welfare are a critical sustainability challenge, yet traditional structural equation modeling (SEM) often assumes population homogeneity, masking distinct resident archetypes and causal pathways. Simultaneously, the proliferation of integrative SEM-clustering methods (e.g., FIMIX-PLS, PLS-POS) creates a methodological imperative for empirical guidance on their comparative performance in capturing unobserved heterogeneity in complex consumer and social systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that different integrative SEM-clustering methods will vary significantly in their ability to recover known, theory-driven population segments and their cluster-specific path models when applied to a complex tourism impact system, with model performance contingent on data characteristics like cluster separation and indicator distribution.
proposals.method: We will collect survey data from residents in diverse heritage tourism destinations, measuring latent constructs like perceived crowding, heritage commodification, community attachment, and subjective welfare. This dataset will serve as a rigorous empirical testbed to benchmark four pre-2026 integrative methods: FIMIX-PLS, PLS-POS, REBUS-PLS, and finite mixture SEM in covariance-based software. Benchmarking criteria will include cluster recovery accuracy (vs. a priori segmentation), model fit indices, interpretability, and stability across subsamples.
proposals.contribution: This research delivers a validated, empirical comparison of cutting-edge heterogeneity detection methods, providing applied researchers in marketing and social sciences with clear guidance for model selection. Substantively, it will generate nuanced, segment-specific policy insights into tourism's adverse effects, moving beyond one-size-fits-all recommendations for sustainable destination management.
proposals.novelty: While method comparisons exist, using a substantively rich, pre-theorized system of tourism impacts as a complex empirical benchmark for these specific techniques is novel. It directly fuses Sarstedt's expertise in measurement validation and methodological pluralism with consumer and social research in a high-stakes applied context.

proposals.title: The Silent Majority and the Vocal Minority: Quantifying Representativeness Bias in User-Generated Content for Consumer Research
proposals.motivation: User-generated content (UGC) is widely mined for consumer insights, sentiment, and brand metrics, often under the implicit assumption that posters are representative of the broader consumer base. However, pre-2026 literature in psychology and marketing suggests that content creation is linked to personality traits like extraversion and need for self-enhancement, potentially skewing data. This bias threatens the validity of conclusions drawn from UGC for strategic decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic differences in demographics, personality traits (specifically extraversion and neuroticism), and brand engagement exist between consumers who generate UGC and those who do not, leading to non-representative UGC data that misrepresents overall consumer sentiment and brand perceptions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-method calibration study. First, we will scrape and analyze UGC (text and metadata) from major brand social media pages using NLP (e.g., LIWC for psycholinguistic features, VADER for sentiment). Second, we will recruit a stratified sample of consumers (posters and non-posters) for the same brands, administering validated surveys measuring personality (BFI-2), brand engagement, and sentiment. We will use propensity score matching and multivariate analysis of variance (MANOVA) to quantify the differences between the groups, calibrating the UGC-derived metrics against the survey-based ground truth.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide empirical evidence and a methodological framework for assessing representativeness bias in UGC. It will advance measurement validation in consumer research by demonstrating how to calibrate UGC metrics, offering practitioners a tool to adjust insights for systematic poster bias and improving the reliability of UGC as a data source.
proposals.novelty: While the existence of a 'vocal minority' is acknowledged, this study directly quantifies the bias by linking observed UGC to the psychographic profiles of its creators versus non-creators, moving beyond demographics to include personality—a key predictor of posting behavior often missing in bias assessments.

proposals.title: Inferring Latent Consumer Mindsets: A Multi-Method Framework for Validating Implicit Constructs in User-Generated Content
proposals.motivation: Current methods for mining consumer mindsets from user-generated content (UGC) rely heavily on the explicit mention of attitudinal constructs (e.g., 'quality,' 'trust'). This limits analysis to what consumers directly articulate, ignoring the rich, implicit psychological drivers that shape behavior but are not explicitly stated in text. This gap is critical for marketing science, as it hinders the accurate measurement of key constructs like perceived value, brand attachment, or post-purchase dissonance from organic data sources.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-method framework combining advanced natural language processing with targeted calibration experiments can validly infer latent, unstated consumer mindset constructs from UGC, significantly improving predictive validity for behavioral outcomes compared to models relying solely on explicit textual markers.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage, mixed-methods design. First, we will use transformer-based language models (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa) fine-tuned on marketing lexicon to analyze large-scale UGC (e.g., reviews, social media posts) and generate probabilistic embeddings representing the semantic context. Second, we will design and conduct online calibration experiments where participants generate UGC in controlled scenarios targeting specific latent constructs (e.g., induced cognitive dissonance). The experimental data will validate and refine the NLP model's ability to map linguistic patterns to the unstated constructs, creating a validated inference algorithm.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, validated methodological framework for extracting a more complete and accurate representation of consumer psychology from UGC. It will advance measurement theory in consumer research by formally integrating experimental calibration with computational text analysis. For practitioners, it offers a more powerful tool for brand and social media analytics that captures the 'why' behind consumer expressions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work mines explicit metrics from UGC or uses multi-methods for validation, our approach uniquely focuses on developing and validating a computational model to infer constructs that are intentionally *not* explicitly mentioned, moving beyond keyword and topic detection to latent psychological state inference.

proposals.title: The Illusion of Precision: A Calibration-Based Validation of NLP Tools for Extracting Nuanced Psychological Constructs from User-Generated Content
proposals.motivation: Consumer research increasingly relies on NLP tools (e.g., LIWC, BERT-based sentiment models) to extract latent psychological constructs (e.g., mindset metrics, brand engagement) from user-generated content. This practice assumes these tools are adequately accurate, yet their validation often lacks rigorous, consumer-specific calibration against established multi-method benchmarks. This creates a validity crisis, potentially leading to flawed conclusions about consumer behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that off-the-shelf NLP tools exhibit significant and systematic measurement error when tasked with extracting nuanced, theory-driven psychological constructs (e.g., extraversion, brand attachment) from social media text, and that their performance varies substantially across different consumer contexts and platforms.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of calibration experiments. First, we will curate benchmark datasets where constructs are measured via established multi-method approaches (e.g., surveys, experimental manipulations). Second, we will apply a suite of NLP tools (from lexicon-based LIWC to pre-2026 transformer architectures like BERT and RoBERTa) to corresponding user-generated text. Third, we will use statistical calibration techniques (e.g., regression-based alignment, Bland-Altman plots) to quantify the discrepancy between NLP-extracted scores and the benchmark, identifying systematic bias and context-dependent error.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorous, methodological framework for validating NLP-based measurement in consumer research, moving beyond simple correlation to diagnose specific error patterns. It will yield concrete guidelines for tool selection and calibration, enhancing the reliability of mining consumer mindset metrics from big data.
proposals.novelty: While multi-method validation is established, applying a formal calibration paradigm from psychometrics and medical testing to assess NLP tools for consumer psychology is novel. It shifts the question from 'do tools correlate?' to 'how and where are they systematically wrong?', directly challenging their assumed adequacy.

proposals.title: Calibrating the Digital Mind: A Multi-Platform Validation of UGC-Derived Consumer Mindset Metrics Against Survey Benchmarks
proposals.motivation: Consumer research increasingly mines user-generated content (UGC) to approximate latent mindset constructs like brand attitude or engagement, promising scale and ecological validity. However, as Sarstedt's recent work on calibration experiments highlights, the validity of such UGC-derived metrics is often assumed rather than rigorously tested against established measurement standards. This creates a critical gap, as the field risks building theory on potentially misaligned operationalizations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that metrics for core consumer mindset constructs (e.g., brand attitude, satisfaction, engagement) derived from a structured four-step UGC analysis process will demonstrate convergent and nomological validity with corresponding traditional survey-based measures, but that the strength of this relationship will vary systematically by platform and brand category.
proposals.method: We will implement the proposed four-step process: 1) Data harvesting of brand-related UGC from Twitter (X) and Instagram via their APIs, 2) Text preprocessing using spaCy and sentiment/emotion lexicons (e.g., VADER, NRC), 3) Feature engineering to create candidate metrics (e.g., sentiment polarity, arousal, topic coherence), and 4) Model calibration using PLS-SEM to map features to latent construct scores. These scores will be validated against survey data from the same users (measured via panel recruitment) for established constructs, using multi-trait multi-method (MTMM) analysis and tests of nomological validity within a larger model.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical benchmarks for the validity of UGC-based mindset measurement, directly answering calls for methodological rigor in computational social science. It will offer concrete guidelines on when and how UGC can reliably approximate specific constructs, advancing the field beyond correlation-based claims toward calibrated measurement.
proposals.novelty: While prior work correlates UGC features with surveys, this study uniquely applies a formal calibration framework—advocated for in traditional research—to the validation of algorithmic metric generation, testing boundary conditions across platforms and contexts in a controlled design.

proposals.title: CALIBRATED MINDSET MINING: Developing and Validating Domain-Specific NLP Tools for Attitudinal Constructs in User-Generated Content
proposals.motivation: The explosion of unstructured user-generated content (UGC) offers unprecedented insight into consumer mindsets, yet existing off-the-shelf NLP tools (e.g., general sentiment analysis) are too coarse to capture specific, theory-driven attitudinal constructs like brand attachment, perceived authenticity, or post-purchase regret. This limits the validity and actionable insights for consumer researchers and marketers. A rigorous, domain-tailored approach is needed to transform UGC into reliable mindset metrics.
proposals.hypothesis: NLP tools specifically fine-tuned and calibrated for distinct attitudinal constructs will extract mindset metrics from UGC with significantly higher convergent and discriminant validity compared to generic sentiment or topic modeling approaches.
proposals.method: We will adopt a multi-stage calibration framework. First, we will curate domain-specific datasets (e.g., social media posts, reviews) and establish ground truth for target constructs (e.g., extraversion, skepticism) via expert coding and established scales. We will then fine-tune pre-trained transformer models (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa) on this labeled data for each construct. Validation will follow Sarstedt's calibration experiments paradigm, using multi-trait multi-method matrices to assess convergent validity against traditional survey measures and discriminant validity against other constructs.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the consumer research community with a validated, open-source toolkit for extracting specific, theory-based mindset metrics from UGC, enhancing measurement validity. It will advance methodological rigor in computational social science by formally integrating psychometric calibration into NLP tool development, moving beyond predictive accuracy to construct validity.
proposals.novelty: While mining UGC is common, this proposal uniquely integrates the calibration experiment methodology from psychometrics into the development of specialized NLP tools, ensuring they measure intended theoretical constructs rather than just lexical patterns. It moves from generic text mining to the creation of validated *measurement instruments* for specific attitudes.

proposals.title: Mapping the Consumer Decision Journey: A Systematic Taxonomy of User-Generated Content Sources Across the Purchase Funnel
proposals.motivation: Marketers and researchers increasingly rely on user-generated content (UGC) to understand consumer behavior, but there is no systematic framework linking specific UGC platforms to distinct stages of the consumer decision-making process. This leads to inefficient data sourcing and potential misalignment between the type of UGC analyzed and the marketing question at hand. A structured mapping is needed to guide researchers and practitioners in selecting the most valid UGC sources for studying need recognition, information search, evaluation, purchase, or post-purchase behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: Different UGC platforms (e.g., Reddit, YouTube, Amazon reviews, Instagram, specialized forums) are systematically associated with and reflect distinct stages of the consumer decision-making process, with their content characteristics (e.g., informational vs. experiential, searchable vs. ephemeral) mapping onto the cognitive and behavioral tasks of each stage.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method approach combining computational text mining and expert validation. First, we will scrape and categorize a large corpus of UGC from major platforms (e.g., using Python's Scrapy and APIs). Second, we will use a pre-trained BERT model fine-tuned on marketing language to classify posts/comments into decision-stage categories (e.g., problem recognition, alternative evaluation) based on linguistic markers. Third, we will validate this computational mapping through a series of calibration experiments with consumer panels, directly testing the diagnostic value of content from each source for inferences about specific decision stages.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first empirically grounded taxonomy linking UGC sources to the consumer decision journey, offering a practical framework for data source selection. It will advance measurement validation in UGC-based research by moving beyond platform-level analysis to a functional, decision-stage perspective. The work will directly inform more precise and valid mining of 'consumer mindset metrics' from digital traces.
proposals.novelty: While prior work mines UGC for insights, it treats platforms monolithically; our approach is novel in deconstructing platforms by the decision-stage utility of their content. Furthermore, we integrate large-scale text mining with calibration experiments—a method championed by Sarstedt for validation—to establish causal links between source and diagnostic value, moving from correlation to justification.

proposals.title: Beyond Sentiment: A Computational Linguistics Framework for Modeling Latent Attitudes and Complex Emotions in Consumer-Generated Text
proposals.motivation: Current consumer research using text data (e.g., social media, reviews) often relies on coarse sentiment analysis, which fails to capture the nuanced attitudes, complex emotional blends, and underlying psychological constructs that drive consumer behavior. This limits the ability to validate mindset metrics and understand the true drivers of electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM) and brand engagement. A deeper, theory-grounded semantic analysis is needed to move from what consumers say to what they truly mean and feel.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying advanced computational linguistics models to user-generated content can reveal latent, multi-dimensional attitude constructs (e.g., authentic enthusiasm vs. obligated praise, cognitive vs. affective trust) that are more predictive of consequential consumer outcomes, such as purchase intent and eWOM sharing, than traditional sentiment scores.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-stage pipeline on large-scale social media and review datasets. First, we will use pre-trained transformer-based language models (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa) fine-tuned for specific consumer psychology tasks to generate rich contextual embeddings. Second, we will apply topic modeling (e.g., BERTopic) and semantic role labeling to deconstruct narratives and identify key attitude objects and actions. Third, we will use dictionary-based and supervised deep learning approaches (e.g., BiLSTM with attention) to quantify specific emotion categories (e.g., awe, frustration) and latent constructs, validating them against established survey-based measures using calibration experiment principles.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated, scalable methodological framework for extracting theory-rich psychological constructs from unstructured text, advancing measurement validation in consumer research. It will offer novel insights into how the semantic structure of eWOM influences its persuasive impact, bridging computational linguistics and consumer psychology.
proposals.novelty: While prior work mines text for metrics, this proposal uniquely integrates state-of-the-art NLP architectures (transformers, role labeling) with consumer theory to model latent, multi-faceted attitudes, moving beyond sentiment polarity to explain the 'why' behind textual expression.

proposals.title: Mapping the Social Fabric of Brands: A Network Analysis of Influence and Attitude Diffusion in User-Generated Content
proposals.motivation: While user-generated content (UGC) is a critical source of consumer mindset metrics, existing methods often treat it as isolated data points, ignoring the social network structures through which brand attitudes are formed and spread. This limits our understanding of how influence flows and which consumers are pivotal in shaping collective brand perceptions. A social network analysis (SNA) approach, grounded in social science, is needed to model these dynamics and move beyond static sentiment analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The diffusion of brand attitudes in UGC is not random but follows a structured network path, where influence is concentrated among a core set of consumers whose structural position (e.g., high betweenness centrality) and content characteristics (e.g., emotional arousal) predict the spread and valence of brand-related discourse.
proposals.method: We will construct a directed interaction network from a large-scale brand-related social media dataset (e.g., Twitter/X or Reddit), linking users based on replies, mentions, and shares. Using Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques (e.g., BERT-based sentiment/attitude classifiers), we will annotate nodes with the attitude valence of their posts. We will then apply SNA algorithms (e.g., community detection via the Louvain method, identification of key influencers using centrality measures like eigenvector and betweenness) and use temporal network models (e.g., SI/SIR models) to trace the diffusion of specific attitudes through the network.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, network-based framework for measuring brand influence, moving beyond individual-level metrics to map the ecosystem of consumer discourse. It will offer managers actionable insights for identifying true opinion leaders and seeding strategies. Theoretically, it bridges social network theory from sociology with consumer research on electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM).
proposals.novelty: While SNA is established in social science, its application to model the *diffusion of brand attitudes* (as opposed to mere information or adoption) within UGC is novel. It integrates network topology with mined psychometric content (attitudes) to explain diffusion patterns, a significant advance over traditional engagement or sentiment analysis.

proposals.title: Real-Time, Global Brand Mindset Tracking: A Scalable Framework for Continuous Consumer Perception Measurement
proposals.motivation: In the pre-2026 digital landscape, brand perception is shaped in real-time by global user-generated content (UGC), yet existing measurement approaches are often static, delayed, and geographically siloed. This creates a critical gap for managers needing to understand dynamic brand health and for researchers studying cross-cultural consumer behavior. A continuous, scalable system is required to move beyond periodic surveys and isolated social media metrics.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-source, multi-lingual UGC processing framework, calibrated against established survey-based brand metrics, can provide valid, real-time indicators of global brand perception that are sensitive to cross-cultural nuances and exogenous events.
proposals.method: We will implement a distributed data pipeline ingesting UGC from global social media and review platforms. Using transformer-based models (e.g., BERT variants) fine-tuned for multilingual sentiment and aspect-based sentiment analysis, we will extract brand mindset metrics (e.g., perceived quality, trust, value). The system will employ streaming architectures (e.g., Apache Kafka/Spark) for real-time processing. Crucially, we will conduct ongoing calibration experiments, as per Sarstedt's prior work, to validate the UGC-derived time-series against traditional panel data across different cultural contexts.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a methodological blueprint for scalable, real-time brand tracking that bridges psychometric rigor and computational social science. It contributes to marketing science by offering a validated, dynamic alternative to static multi-method studies and advances consumer research by enabling the study of global brand perception as a continuous, event-responsive construct.
proposals.novelty: While UGC mining is established, the novelty lies in the integration of continuous calibration for measurement validation within a real-time, global operational system, moving from proof-of-concept studies to a persistent measurement infrastructure grounded in psychometric theory.

ideas.title: A Multi-Cluster Structural Model of Heritage Value Creation via Digital and AI Platforms
ideas.description: This research develops a novel structural equation model to empirically investigate non-human capital pathways for heritage value creation. The model treats clusters (e.g., different heritage types, regions, or institutional settings) as groups with some shared (global) and some unique (cluster-specific) structural path coefficients. Crucially, it hypothesizes that paths from digital platform engagement or AI-driven curation to economic outcomes are global, stable forces, whereas paths from human capital are modeled as cluster-specific, testing their contingent and potentially diminished role. This allows for a unified, cross-contextual validation of new value creation theories while accounting for heterogeneity in traditional factors.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Integrative SEM-Clustering Methods to Identify Heterogeneous Tourism Impact Pathways on Heritage and Welfare
ideas.description: This research fuses the substantive problem of modeling tourism's long-term dissipation of heritage value and welfare with the methodological challenge of comparing simultaneous SEM-clustering approaches. It proposes using the multi-faceted tourism impact system—with its latent variables (e.g., perceived heritage value, resident welfare) and hypothesized heterogeneous population segments (e.g., different resident archetypes, tourist destination types)—as a rigorous, complex empirical test case. By applying and benchmarking multiple integrative methods (e.g., FIMIX-PLS, PLS-POS, REBUS-PLS, finite mixture SEM) to the same dataset, the study will identify which techniques best capture the distinct causal pathways and cluster-specific dynamics of tourism's adverse effects. The synergy yields validated methodological guidance for social science modeling of complex systems while generating nuanced, typology-specific policy insights for sustainable tourism management that a single-model approach could not provide.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery for Micro-Heterogeneity in Heritage Site Performance
ideas.description: This research applies causal structure discovery to the micro-level analysis of individual museums or natural parks, treating each site as a heterogeneous unit. By integrating this with clustering, the method identifies groups of sites that share not just similar observable characteristics, but similar underlying causal mechanisms linking their territorial capital assets (e.g., collections, location, community ties) to outcomes like visitor engagement or financial sustainability. This bypasses the need for a one-size-fits-all causal model or pre-specified typologies, revealing how the causal pathways to success or failure differ across emergent, data-driven clusters. The result is a diagnostic tool for heritage managers that pinpoints the most effective leverage points for intervention specific to their site's causal profile.

ideas.title: Mapping the Heritage Political Economy: A Stakeholder-Centric Model of Value Co-Creation and Capture
ideas.description: This research moves beyond the local/tourist dichotomy to model heritage as a dynamic political-economic system. It investigates how different stakeholders—including local communities, tourists, policymakers, and commercial entities—interact to co-create, legitimize, and capture value from heritage. The methodology combines network analysis of stakeholder influence with longitudinal case studies to trace financial and cultural flows. The goal is to produce a diagnostic framework that identifies governance models where heritage valorization genuinely enhances local agency, social cohesion, and economic resilience, rather than merely extracting value for external markets.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Simultaneous Clustering and PLS-SEM via Phylogenetic Tree Construction Methods
ideas.description: This idea extends the simultaneous clustering and PLS-SEM methodology to hierarchical segmentation structures by drawing an analogy from computational biology. In phylogenetics, methods like Bayesian phylogenetic inference simultaneously estimate the hierarchical tree (phylogeny) and the parameters of the evolutionary model from sequence data. Adapting this solution, we propose to develop a co-estimation algorithm for marketing or social science data. This algorithm would perform a stochastic search (e.g., via a tailored MCMC or efficient heuristic) through the joint space of possible hierarchical cluster trees and the PLS-SEM path coefficients for each cluster at each level of the hierarchy. The procedure would optimize a criterion that trades off the goodness-of-fit of the PLS models within clusters against the complexity of the hierarchical segmentation, effectively building a 'phylogeny' of consumer or respondent segments based on their structural equation model similarities.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Theory to Heritage Value Creation
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that human capital is the sole creator of economic value in heritage by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, the concept of 'ecosystem engineers' (e.g., beavers creating ponds, corals building reefs) demonstrated that non-mobile, non-animal actors can be primary architects of value-creating systems, structuring environments for entire biological communities. The analogous solution involved analyzing energy flows, nutrient cycles, and physical modifications to quantify the total economic value generated by these engineered systems. Adapted to heritage, this approach treats digital platforms, AI algorithms, and automated archival systems as 'heritage ecosystem engineers.' The research would investigate how these non-human actors structure the heritage 'ecosystem'—curating attention flows, enabling new user interactions, and creating economic niches (e.g., micro-licensing, predictive maintenance, automated content generation for tourism). The goal is to map and quantify the 'value webs' they generate, such as an AI recommendation system directing visitor traffic and spending to previously overlooked sites, thereby demonstrating economic value creation decoupled from direct human capital input.

ideas.title: Latent Class Analysis of Heritage-Tourism Systems to Identify and Model Enhancement Pathways
ideas.description: This research applies finite mixture modeling and latent class analysis to the study of cultural and natural heritage sites under tourist pressure. By analyzing multivariate data on conservation status, tourist management regimes, community involvement, and economic indicators, the model identifies latent classes of site-tourism systems without pre-assigning them as 'positive' or 'negative.' This allows for a formal, statistical test of the inverted assumption by quantifying the probability that a site belongs to a class where tourism metrics are positively associated with enhanced heritage conservation, thereby revealing the specific configurations of factors that lead to synergistic outcomes versus dissipative ones.

ideas.title: Apply Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology to quantify the full impact chain of heritage tourism
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology from Industrial Ecology to heritage tourism. The analogous solution is the use of standardized LCA to map all environmental inputs and outputs of a product system and assign them to impact categories. Adapted to this context, the research would treat a heritage site as a service system and conduct a full LCA-style analysis. This involves creating an inventory of all resource consumption (energy, water, materials) and emissions/waste generation linked to tourism operations and site maintenance, then translating this inventory into quantifiable environmental footprint metrics for the site. This provides a rigorous, systemic method to assess and manage the resource dissipation of cultural-natural heritage, similar to how LCA is used to improve the sustainability of manufactured products.

ideas.title: Causal Modeling of Tourist Demand Levers on Heritage Integrity Thresholds
ideas.description: This research moves beyond the assumption of 'good management' to empirically identify the specific causal mechanisms through which tourist demand can be harnessed for conservation. It employs a longitudinal, multi-site study design that establishes precise, measurable baselines for cultural and natural heritage integrity. By treating tourist volume, pricing, spatial distribution, and revenue allocation as independent variables, the system tests which combinations trigger positive, neutral, or negative shifts in conservation outcomes against predefined integrity thresholds. The result is a predictive model that defines the operational conditions under which tourism transitions from a threat to a sustainable conservation finance and stewardship tool.

ideas.title: Adversarial Simulation Framework for Robustness Testing of PLS-SEM Methodology on Complex Models
ideas.description: This research designs a simulation study to adversarially evaluate the proposed methodology on complex PLS-SEM models. Instead of merely applying it to correctly specified models, the framework intentionally introduces realistic pathologies like omitted variable bias, cross-loadings, and incorrectly specified moderation effects. The core outcome is a rigorous assessment of the methodology's failure modes, diagnostic sensitivity, and boundary conditions under which it provides reliable or misleading results, thereby delivering a substantially more robust and practically useful validation.

ideas.title: Simultaneous Clustering and Optimization for Heritage Tourism Carrying Capacity using Covariance-Based SEM
ideas.description: This research fuses the extension of simultaneous clustering logic to covariance-based Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with the dual problem of optimizing tourist demand under heritage conservation constraints. The result is a novel analytical framework that uses covariance-based SEM to model the complex structural relationships between tourist activity, economic welfare, and heritage asset depreciation. The simultaneous clustering component is applied to identify latent, homogeneous groups of heritage sites or tourist markets based on their underlying covariance structures. For each cluster, the model then solves the dual optimization problem to determine the unique tourist carrying capacity that maximizes welfare without triggering heritage dissipation, providing tailored sustainability thresholds for different types of sites or demand patterns.

ideas.title: Quantifying the AI-Augmented Heritage Economy: A Comparative Growth Contribution Analysis
ideas.description: This research fuses the comparative growth analysis of Idea A with the non-human capital value creation of Idea B to empirically test a new hypothesis: that AI and digital platforms can transform heritage into a scalable, capital-intensive economic sector. It will develop metrics to formally compare the growth, productivity, and employment contributions of this 'AI-augmented heritage economy' against traditional manufacturing and standard tourism sectors. The study aims to determine if digital automation enables heritage to generate economic value in a manner that parallels or diverges from historical industrial pathways, challenging assumptions about the fundamental drivers of development in service-based economies.

ideas.title: Micro-Segmented Analysis of Heritage Site Capital with Partial Homogeneity
ideas.description: This research applies the territorial capital framework at the micro-scale of a single museum or natural park but models its internal heterogeneity. It uses segmentation (e.g., by visitor type, stakeholder group, or spatial zone within the site) but relaxes the assumption that all structural relationships between capital dimensions must differ between segments. Instead, it employs partial homogeneity modeling to test which specific paths in the structural model (e.g., from social to cultural capital) are invariant and which are segment-specific. This allows for precise, actionable insights into how different groups experience and contribute to the site's multifaceted capital, supporting targeted interventions without overfitting or losing generalizable principles of site dynamics.

ideas.title: Applying Predator-Prey Dynamics to Model Tourist-Heritage Coexistence
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from ecology and population biology. The analogous problem is the predator-prey dynamic, where unchecked predation leads to collapse, but well-managed interaction can create a stable, enhancing equilibrium for both populations, as modeled by Lotka-Volterra equations and observed in trophic cascades. The adapted idea is to model the tourist-heritage relationship similarly, testing if well-managed tourist demand (the 'predator') can enhance, not dissipate, cultural and natural heritage conservation (the 'prey'). By defining and controlling key variables like interaction rate (visitor volume/behavior) and carrying capacity (site resilience), the research aims to identify management regimes where tourism provides essential conservation resources—funding, political priority, and community stewardship—creating a sustainable, mutually reinforcing system that inverts the default assumption of negative impact.

ideas.title: Statistically-Validated Partial Path Heterogeneity in Segmented Structural Models
ideas.description: This approach introduces a rigorous statistical testing protocol to determine which specific model paths can validly differ across latent segments. The system begins with a fully heterogeneous baseline model and uses penalized estimation to shrink differences in non-informative paths, generating a candidate set of potentially heterogeneous parameters. It then applies a confirmatory, multi-step testing procedure—controlling for false discovery rates—to retain only those path differences supported by strong statistical evidence. The final model enforces homogeneity for all non-significant paths while allowing validated heterogeneity in others, resulting in a parsimonious and replicable segmentation structure grounded in formal inference rather than exploratory relaxation.

ideas.title: Benchmarking PLS-SEM Clustering via a Multi-Model Tournament Framework
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational ecology, where new species distribution models are benchmarked using multi-criteria tournament frameworks (e.g., BIOMOD), to the context of SEM mixture methods. The analogous solution involves running multiple competing models on standardized data and evaluating them across a suite of performance metrics to determine context-specific superiority. The adapted idea is to design and execute a methodological 'tournament' where the new PLS-SEM clustering method is benchmarked against a range of existing covariance-based SEM mixture models (like FIMIX-PLS and various latent class SEM techniques). The benchmark will use simulated datasets (with known ground-truth clusters and parameter values) and relevant real-world datasets, evaluating competitors on a comprehensive set of criteria including cluster recovery fidelity, parameter estimation bias, predictive power for out-of-sample data, model fit indices, and computational scalability. The goal is to move beyond declaring a single 'best' method and instead produce a diagnostic framework that identifies the optimal methodological choice based on data characteristics (e.g., sample size, distribution, model complexity, presence of heterogeneity).

ideas.title: Ensemble Clustering for Robust Agent-Based Simulation of Tourist Segments and Congestion Dynamics
ideas.description: This research integrates ensemble clustering methods with agent-based modeling (ABM) to create a robust, multi-scale simulation of tourist congestion. First, ensemble clustering is applied to tourist survey or transactional data to identify stable, reproducible behavioral segments (e.g., budget planners, luxury spontaneists). These validated segments then define the heterogeneous agent types and their decision rules within the ABM, which simulates their spatial-temporal movements and interactions. The synergy allows for testing how different stable tourist segments contribute disproportionately to congestion or economic impact, and for evaluating policy interventions targeted at specific behavioral profiles within the simulated system.

ideas.title: Causal Attribution and Dynamic Systems Modeling of Tourism-Induced Heritage and Welfare Erosion
ideas.description: This research employs a causal inference approach to isolate and quantify the specific impact of tourism from other confounding factors on heritage value and local welfare. It constructs synthetic counterfactuals for heritage sites, comparing trajectories of similar locations that experienced different tourism growth patterns to establish a causal baseline. The system then integrates these causal estimates into a dynamic simulation model that captures reinforcing feedback loops, such as how welfare decline alters community stewardship of heritage. The output is a validated, policy-testing tool that can project outcomes under different intervention scenarios, moving beyond description to predictive, actionable insight.

ideas.title: Allow for non-normal or categorical data

ideas.title: Participatory Multi-Agent Simulation for Robust Heritage-Tourism Governance Under Deep Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research develops an agent-based modeling and participatory simulation framework to explore sustainable tourism pathways for heritage sites. Instead of seeking a single optimal demand level, it models the interactions and conflicting preferences of resident communities, tourism operators, visitors, and regulatory bodies, each with distinct perceptions of welfare and heritage impact. The system employs exploratory modeling and robust decision-making techniques to stress-test management strategies against a wide range of uncertain futures and stakeholder value systems. The outcome is a set of adaptive governance insights that identify trade-offs, potential coalitions, and interventions resilient to disagreement and uncertainty about heritage dissipation dynamics.

ideas.title: Challenge need for pre-specified causal structure

ideas.title: Latent Class Growth Modeling of Heritage Site Ecological Footprint Trajectories
ideas.description: This research fuses ecological footprint analysis with longitudinal mixture modeling to identify and characterize latent classes of cultural-natural heritage sites based on their unique trajectories of resource consumption and waste dissipation over time. Instead of a single snapshot, it applies latent class growth modeling (LCGM) to repeated ecological footprint measurements, clustering sites into groups that share similar patterns of environmental impact evolution. This allows for the prediction of future sustainability pressures for different site typologies and the development of targeted, time-sensitive management interventions for each latent class, transforming footprint analysis from a descriptive accounting tool into a predictive, classification-based framework for strategic heritage conservation.

ideas.title: Modeling Heritage Degradation as a Metabolic Disease: A Systems Dynamics Approach from Epidemiology
ideas.description: This research adapts systems dynamics modeling from epidemiology to quantify tourism's adverse effects. The analogous problem is modeling the progression of a chronic metabolic disease, where complex feedback loops determine long-term health outcomes. The solution involves mapping the heritage-tourism system's key variables (e.g., tourist density, infrastructure strain, resident satisfaction, conservation investment, physical degradation) and their causal interrelationships into a formal systems dynamics model. This model will simulate the long-term dissipation of heritage value and welfare decline, treating tourist congestion and resource consumption as pathogenic stressors. By identifying and testing intervention points (analogous to therapeutic targets), the research aims to predict which policy mixes can shift the system from a pathological state of degradation to a sustainable state of health.

ideas.title: Automated model generation from textual hypotheses

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Compare Sectoral Growth Contributions
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecology, where scientists formally compare the contributions of different species to ecosystem function, to the economic problem of comparing tourism and manufacturing. In ecology, niche modeling quantifies how different taxa contribute to productivity, resilience, and functional roles within a community. The adapted idea applies this framework by modeling tourism and manufacturing as occupying distinct 'economic niches.' The research would empirically test and compare each sector's resource use efficiency (e.g., capital investment per job), contribution to economic 'productivity' (GDP growth), and resilience/stability (employment and output volatility) in response to external shocks. This provides a formal, multidimensional comparison of their growth contributions, moving beyond simple output or job counts to assess their functional roles and potential redundancy within the national economic system.

ideas.title: A Participatory Pressure-State-Response Framework for Adaptive Management of Heritage Site Footprints
ideas.description: This research moves beyond mere footprint quantification to establish a dynamic, stakeholder-driven governance system for heritage tourism sustainability. It integrates ecological footprint analysis with local knowledge and management priorities within a Pressure-State-Response (PSR) model, co-developed with site managers, community representatives, and policymakers. The system continuously links measured resource pressures (footprint) to observed site states and pre-negotiated, feasible response actions, enabling adaptive management. This transforms footprint data from a static report into a living decision-support tool that directly informs and iteratively improves conservation and tourism policy.

ideas.title: Extending Simultaneous Clustering to Covariance-Based SEM via Image Segmentation Logic
ideas.description: The original idea proposes extending simultaneous clustering logic from variance-based SEM to covariance-based and other methods. Drawing an analogy from medical imaging and computer vision, where image segmentation algorithms (e.g., region-growing) solve the parallel problem of jointly partitioning data (pixels into regions) and estimating region parameters. The analogous solution is a joint optimization framework solved iteratively. Adapting this, the research idea is to develop a new methodological framework for covariance-based SEM that simultaneously performs clustering of observations and estimation of multiple group SEM parameters. This would involve creating an iterative algorithm (e.g., an EM variant) that alternates between assigning observations to clusters based on their model-implied covariance fit and re-estimating the cluster-specific structural models, thereby generalizing the simultaneous clustering logic to a broader class of SEM techniques.

ideas.title: CB-SEM with non-normal data without corrections
ideas.description: Develop robust CB-SEM estimators that do not rely on distributional assumptions or asymptotic corrections.
ideas.sourceTitle: Covariance-based structural equation modeling (CB-SEM): a SmartPLS 4 software tutorial

ideas.title: Ensemble Clustering for Robust Heritage-Carrying Capacity Estimation
ideas.description: This research synthesizes ensemble clustering with the dual problem of optimizing tourist demand under capital constraints. It applies bootstrap ensemble clustering to multidimensional data on tourist flows, site conditions, and local welfare, generating not a single optimal demand level but a robust distribution of plausible 'carrying capacity' clusters. This allows policymakers to visualize the stability of different demand regimes and identify demand levels that are consistently associated with high welfare and low heritage dissipation across many bootstrap samples. The result is a data-driven, uncertainty-quantified tool for setting tourist caps that are resilient to measurement error and model variability.

ideas.title: Robust Hierarchical Segmentation with PLS-SEM: A Stability-Validated Framework Using Data Splitting and Equivalence Testing
ideas.description: This research develops a rigorous methodology for integrating hierarchical clustering with PLS-SEM that directly addresses statistical circularity and instability. The framework employs a split-sample or multi-sample design where one subset discovers the hierarchical segment structure using stability-based criteria, another estimates the PLS-SEM models within each confirmed segment, and a holdout sample validates the predictive utility of the entire hierarchical model. The approach includes formal tests for the reliability of dendrogram nodes and statistical equivalence of path coefficients across branches, ensuring that the resulting hierarchical segmentation is a robust feature of the population rather than a sample-specific artifact.

ideas.title: Information-Theoretic Model Selection for Overlapping Clusters with Varying Degrees of Sharing
ideas.description: This research develops novel information criteria for fuzzy or probabilistic clustering models where observations can belong to multiple clusters. The core challenge is that traditional criteria, designed for crisp partitions, fail to account for the complexity and redundancy introduced by overlapping membership. The new criteria will penalize model complexity not just by the number of clusters, but by the effective degrees of freedom in the membership matrix, balancing fit against the risk of over-parameterized, highly overlapping clusters. This enables the simultaneous, data-driven determination of both the optimal number of latent segments and the optimal *pattern of sharing* between them, providing a principled way to choose between a model with many distinct clusters and one with fewer, but more overlapping, clusters.

ideas.title: Transfer uncertainty budgets from engineering metrology

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Resilience Testing to Validate PLS-SEM Model Complexity
ideas.description: This expanded idea draws an analogy from Systems Ecology, where complex ecosystem models are validated by introducing controlled perturbations to assess resilience and uncover hidden dynamics. The analogous solution involves stress-testing models by simulating shocks (e.g., species removal) to see if predicted feedbacks and indirect effects materialize. Adapted to PLS-SEM research, this translates to a future work proposal: develop and apply a 'perturbation-testing' simulation framework for PLS-SEM models with moderating or mediating effects. The methodology would involve systematically varying key parameters (like moderator levels or mediator strengths) in controlled, extreme, or unexpected combinations within a Monte Carlo simulation. The goal is to evaluate whether the proposed methodology for complex PLS-SEM structures robustly captures the theorized interactions under stress, identifying conditions where model inferences may break down or reveal instability not apparent in standard calibration.

ideas.title: Scale calibration to high-throughput screening
